Iʿrāb — word by word دِينُsubject, possessing the next · مُبْتَدَأ · مُضَافمَرْفُوع اللهِpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور وَاحِدٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
09
رَجَعْتُ إِلَى بَيْتِي.

I returned to my house.

Notice: بَيْتِي — the noun is after a preposition, so it wants a kasra; but the ـِي suffix has already claimed the ending. When "my" attaches, the case ending simply disappears beneath it.

Iʿrāb — word by word رَجَعْتُverb + I · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل— إِلَىpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— بَيْتِيafter the preposition + my · اِسْم مَجْرُور + ضَمِيرمَجْرُور
10
رَبَّنَا، آتِنَا فِي الدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً.

Our Lord, give us good in this world.

When you hear it: the beginning of one of the most-recited duʿās in the Qur'an. رَبَّنَا = رَبّ + ـنَا: our Lord.

Iʿrāb — word by word رَبَّنَاthe called-upon — vocative takes naṣb · مُنَادَىمَنْصُوب آتِنَاcommand, defective + us · فِعْل أَمْر + مَفْعُول بِه— فِيpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الدُّنْيَاafter the preposition — the kasra hides on the ā · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور حَسَنَةًsecond object · مَفْعُول بِه ثَانٍمَنْصُوب

Section 4Grammar — The Iḍāfa and the Attached Pronouns

Part one: the possession chain

English says the book of the student, or the student's book. Arabic says neither. It simply places the two nouns next to each other:

كِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ

the student's book · the book of the student

Two words, no glue. And the rules are exactly three:

RuleWhat it means
1. The second noun is always in jarrAlways. No exceptions. That kasra on الطَّالِبِ is not optional — it is the possession.
2. The first noun takes whatever case its job requiresDoer → ḍamma. Object → fatḥa. After a preposition → kasra.
3. The first noun never takes الـ and never takes tanwīnIt cannot. It is already made definite by the word after it.
Rule 3 is the one that gives learners away

You cannot say الْكِتَابُ الطَّالِبِ. Never. The moment a noun enters a chain, it strips off its الـ and its tanwīn — because it has borrowed definiteness from the noun behind it.

This is also your listening cue: a noun with no الـ and no -un tail, followed by a word ending in -i, is an iḍāfa. Every time.

Chains can be as long as you like

مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ

Master of the Day of Judgement.

Three nouns, one chain. Only the last one is "free"; everything before it has surrendered its الـ, and everything after the first is in jarr. Arabic will happily stack four or five deep, and classical prose does so constantly. Once you hear the chain, you hear the whole phrase as a single unit — which is precisely what fluent listening feels like.

Part two: the attached pronouns

Arabic has no separate word for my, your, his. It attaches a small tail to the noun — and that tail behaves as the second half of an iḍāfa.

SuffixMeaningOn a nounOn a verb / preposition
ـِيmy / meكِتَابِي my bookعِنْدِي I have
ـنَاour / usرَبُّنَا our Lordعَلَّمَنَا he taught us
ـكَyour / you (m.)كِتَابُكَ your bookعِنْدَكَ you have
ـكِyour / you (f.)كِتَابُكِعِنْدَكِ
ـهُhis / himكِتَابُهُ his bookسَأَلْتُهُ I asked him
ـهَاher / itكِتَابُهَاقَرَأْتُهَا I read it
ـهُمْtheir / themقُلُوبُهُمْ their heartsسَأَلْتُهُمْ I asked them
ـكُمْyour / you (pl.)رَبُّكُمْ your Lordعِنْدَكُمْ you (pl.) have
The single most useful thing in this chapter

These same eight suffixes work on nouns, on verbs, and on prepositions — and the meaning shifts automatically:

كِتَابُهُ — his book.  ·  سَأَلْتُهُ — I asked him.  ·  عَنْهُ — about him.

One set of tails. Three jobs. You have just learned the possessive, the object pronoun, and the prepositional pronoun of Arabic — all at once, in a single table.

Watch the vowel bend

One small piece of music. After a kasra or a ي, the suffixes ـهُ and ـهُمْ soften their vowel to a kasra:

كِتَابُهُ  →  فِي كِتَابِهِ

his book → in his book (-hu becomes -hi)

Do not memorise this. Just notice it happening, and let your mouth copy it. It is the language smoothing itself out, exactly as English turns a apple into an apple.

Section 5Reading Practice

بَيْتُ الْعِلْمِ

الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ.

هٰذَا بَيْتُ الشَّيْخِ. فِي بَيْتِهِ كُتُبٌ كَثِيرَةٌ، وَفِي قَلْبِهِ عِلْمٌ كَثِيرٌ.

يَجْلِسُ أَهْلُ الْعِلْمِ فِي بَيْتِهِ، وَيَسْأَلُونَهُ عَنْ دِينِهِمْ. وَهُوَ يُجِيبُهُمْ.

قَالَ لَهُمْ: كِتَابُ اللهِ أَوَّلًا، ثُمَّ سُنَّةُ رَسُولِهِ. هٰذَا دِينُنَا.

ثُمَّ رَجَعُوا إِلَى بُيُوتِهِمْ، وَفِي قُلُوبِهِمْ نُورٌ.

The House of Knowledge

All praise is for Allah, Lord of the worlds.

This is the shaykh's house. In his house are many books, and in his heart is much knowledge.

The people of knowledge sit in his house and ask him about their religion. And he answers them.

He said to them: the Book of Allah first, then the sunnah of His Messenger. This is our religion.

Then they returned to their houses, and in their hearts was a light.

New in context: أَوَّلًا firstly · سُنَّة the way, the practice · يُجِيبُ he answers · لَهُمْ to them

Count the chains

رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ · بَيْتُ الشَّيْخِ · أَهْلُ الْعِلْمِ · كِتَابُ اللهِ · سُنَّةُ رَسُولِهِ — five iḍāfas in one short passage, and that is not unusual. It is the ordinary texture of religious Arabic.

And notice سُنَّةُ رَسُولِهِ: a chain whose second half is itself carrying a possessive suffix. The sunnah of His Messenger. The machinery nests without complaint.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ، وَالصَّلَاةُ وَالسَّلَامُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِ اللهِ.

أَيُّهَا الطُّلَّابُ، سَأَلَنِي رَجُلٌ أَمْسِ عَنْ قَلْبِهِ. قَالَ: يَا شَيْخُ، قَلْبِي مَرِيضٌ. مَاذَا أَعْمَلُ؟

فَقُلْتُ لَهُ: دَوَاءُ الْقَلْبِ فِي كِتَابِ رَبِّكَ. اِقْرَأْهُ، وَاعْمَلْ بِهِ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَمَاذَا عَنْ عَمَلِنَا؟ نَحْنُ نَقْرَأُ، وَلٰكِنْ لَا نَعْمَلُ.

الشَّيْخُ:

سُؤَالُكَ هُوَ الْجَوَابُ، يَا بُنَيَّ. الْعِلْمُ بِلَا عَمَلٍ شَجَرَةٌ بِلَا ثَمَرٍ.

Shaykh: All praise is for Allah, and peace and blessings upon the Messenger of Allah. Students — a man asked me yesterday about his heart. He said: Shaykh, my heart is sick. What should I do? So I said to him: the medicine of the heart is in the Book of your Lord. Read it, and act by it.

Student: Shaykh — and what about our action? We read, but we do not act.

Shaykh: Your question is the answer, my son. Knowledge without action is a tree without fruit.

New: أَيُّهَا O (addressing) · أَمْسِ yesterday · مَرِيض sick · دَوَاء medicine · الْجَوَاب the answer · بُنَيَّ my son · بِلَا without · شَجَرَة tree · ثَمَر fruit

Say it yourself

هٰذَا كِتَابِي.

سَأَلْتُهُ عَنْ دِينِهِ.

رَجَعْتُ إِلَى بَيْتِي.

Section 7Word Sheets

Two verbs today — but two of the most consequential in the language.

verb عَبَدَ
maṣdar عِبَادَة worship
to worship, to serve
Form I · sound · يَعْبُدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIعَبَدْتُأَعْبُدُ——
نَحْنُweعَبَدْنَانَعْبُدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)عَبَدْتَتَعْبُدُاُعْبُدْلَا تَعْبُدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)عَبَدْتِتَعْبُدِينَاُعْبُدِيلَا تَعْبُدِي
هُوَheعَبَدَيَعْبُدُ——
هِيَsheعَبَدَتْتَعْبُدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)عَبَدْتُمَاتَعْبُدَانِاُعْبُدَالَا تَعْبُدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)عَبَدْتُمْتَعْبُدُونَاُعْبُدُوالَا تَعْبُدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)عَبَدْتُمَاتَعْبُدَانِاُعْبُدَالَا تَعْبُدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)عَبَدْتُنَّتَعْبُدْنَاُعْبُدْنَلَا تَعْبُدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)عَبَدَايَعْبُدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)عَبَدُوايَعْبُدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)عَبَدَتَاتَعْبُدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)عَبَدْنَيَعْبُدْنَ——

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ — “You alone we worship.” That is the نَحْنُ row of this very grid, from Sūrat al-Fātiḥah. And عَبْد, “servant,” is the same three letters in a noun-mould.

verb حَمِدَ
maṣdar حَمْد praise
to praise
Form I · sound · يَحْمَدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIحَمِدْتُأَحْمَدُ——
نَحْنُweحَمِدْنَانَحْمَدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)حَمِدْتَتَحْمَدُاِحْمَدْلَا تَحْمَدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)حَمِدْتِتَحْمَدِينَاِحْمَدِيلَا تَحْمَدِي
هُوَheحَمِدَيَحْمَدُ——
هِيَsheحَمِدَتْتَحْمَدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)حَمِدْتُمَاتَحْمَدَانِاِحْمَدَالَا تَحْمَدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)حَمِدْتُمْتَحْمَدُونَاِحْمَدُوالَا تَحْمَدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)حَمِدْتُمَاتَحْمَدَانِاِحْمَدَالَا تَحْمَدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)حَمِدْتُنَّتَحْمَدْنَاِحْمَدْنَلَا تَحْمَدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)حَمِدَايَحْمَدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)حَمِدُوايَحْمَدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)حَمِدَتَاتَحْمَدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)حَمِدْنَيَحْمَدْنَ——

This root generates more names than any other in the language: حَامِد (one who praises) · مَحْمُود (one who is praised) · أَحْمَد · مُحَمَّد (one praised abundantly). And the maṣdar opens the Qur'an: الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ.

And the nouns

رَبّlord, master, sustainer
Plural: أَرْبَاب
Root family: رَبَّىٰ he raised, nurtured · تَرْبِيَة upbringing, education · رَبَّانِيّ devoted to the Lord
The root means to nurture something to completion. A رَبّ is not merely a master — He is the one who brings a thing, step by step, to what it was meant to be.
قَلْبheart
Plural: قُلُوب
Root family: قَلَبَ he turned over · تَقَلُّب fluctuation · اِنْقِلَاب a coup, an overturning
دِينreligion, way of life, judgement
Plural: أَدْيَان
Root family: دَانَ he judged, he owed · دَيْن a debt · مَدِين indebted
Religion, judgement, and debt from one root. The theology is doing the etymology.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · active المضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itself اسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passive المضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-to الأمرcommand
to worshipعَبَدَيَعْبُدُعِبَادَةعَابِدعُبِدَيُعْبَدُمَعْبُوداُعْبُدْ
to praiseحَمِدَيَحْمَدُحَمْدحَامِدحُمِدَيُحْمَدُمَحْمُوداِحْمَدْ
One root through all eight moulds — عَبَدَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ع ب د, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. عَبَدَ اللهَ وَحْدَهُ. — He worshipped Allah alone.

المضارع · present active. يَعْبُدُ الْمُؤْمِنُ رَبَّهُ بِالْحُبِّ وَالْخَوْفِ. — The believer worships his Lord with love and fear.

المصدر · the action itself. الصَّلَاةُ عِبَادَةٌ عَظِيمَةٌ. — Prayer is a great act of worship.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْعَابِدُ بِلَا عِلْمٍ يَضِلُّ. — The worshipper without knowledge goes astray.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. عُبِدَ الصَّنَمُ فِي الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ. — The idol was worshipped in the age of ignorance.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. لَا يُعْبَدُ بِحَقٍّ إِلَّا اللهُ. — None is rightfully worshipped but Allah.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. لَا مَعْبُودَ بِحَقٍّ إِلَّا اللهُ. — There is no true object of worship but Allah.

الأمر · command. اُعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ. — Worship your Lord until certainty comes to you.

Read the second row across

حَمِدَ → حَامِد (one who praises) → مَحْمُود (one who is praised) → and, from an intensified form of the same root, مُحَمَّد (one praised abundantly) and أَحْمَد.

Four of the most-spoken names in human history, and they are all the same three letters, poured into different moulds. You did not learn them. You derived them.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

الرَّبُّthe Lord الْعَبْدُthe servant عَبَدَhe worshipped حَمِدَhe praised الدِّينُthe religion الْقَلْبُthe heart الْبَيْتُthe house الِاسْمُthe name الْأَبُthe father الِابْنُthe son الْأَرْضُthe earth السَّمَاءُthe sky الرَّحْمَةُthe mercy الْأُمَّةُthe community أَهْلُpeople of

Spiral recap — Chapter 1, fourth pass

These are due today on the schedule. If they come instantly, they are yours for life.

قَالَhe said هٰذَاthis (m.) الَّذِيwho / which عَنْabout / from النَّاسُthe people شَيْءٌthing كَثِيرٌmany مُهِمٌّimportant
What you can now do

Read this, and parse every word: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ. الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ.

Preposition + iḍāfa. Nominal sentence. Iḍāfa. Every ending explained, every role named. Four days ago, that was calligraphy. Today it is grammar.

Before you close the book

Sixty words. Two tenses. Three sentence frames. And the possession chain — which is, quietly, the single most common structure in the Arabic you actually want to hear. From here on, when a shaykh strings four nouns together, you will not hear a wall of sound. You will hear a chain, and you will know exactly where it starts and where it ends.

End of Chapter 4 Next → Describing: How Arabic Says "Good"
Day 05 · Grammar: the adjective & agreement

Describing:
The Follower

In Arabic an adjective is called al-naʿt — "the follower." It does not stand beside its noun. It obeys it, in four separate ways at once. Get those four right and your Arabic will sound native. Get one wrong and it will not.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Make an adjective agree with its noun in all four respects: definiteness, gender, number, and case.
  2. Understand — finally and completely — why الْبَيْتُ الْكَبِيرُ and الْبَيْتُ كَبِيرٌ are different things, and be able to hear the difference at speed.
  3. Learn the فَعِيل mould, which generates a huge share of the adjectives in Islamic vocabulary — and most of the Names of Allah.
  4. Handle the strange rule that non-human plurals are treated as feminine singular.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01كَبِير
big, great, elder
adjectiveك – ب – رmould فَعِيل
اللهُ أَكْبَرُ.

Allah is greater.

أَكْبَر is the comparative of this adjective — the mould أَفْعَل. You have said this word thousands of times; now you know its grammar.

02صَغِير
small, young
adjectiveص – غ – رmould فَعِيل
هٰذَا بَيْتٌ صَغِيرٌ.

This is a small house.

03جَدِيد
new
adjectiveج – د – دmould فَعِيل
حَفِظْتُ كَلِمَاتٍ جَدِيدَةً.

I memorised new words.

04قَدِيم
old, ancient
adjectiveق – د – مmould فَعِيل
قَرَأْتُ كِتَابًا قَدِيمًا.

I read an old book.

Used of things, not people — an old man is كَبِير or شَيْخ, never قَدِيم.

05صَالِح
righteous, sound, fit
adjectiveص – ل – حthe doer-mould فَاعِل
آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ.

They believed and did righteous deeds.

This is the fāʿil mould you know from the derivation tables, being used as an adjective. That is normal: the doer-noun and the adjective are the same shape, and context tells you which is meant.

06طَيِّب
good, pure, wholesome
adjectiveط – ي – بQur'anic
كَلِمَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ.

A good word.

Carries purity, not just quality: حَلَالٌ طَيِّبٌ — lawful and wholesome. Also the everyday “okay / fine” in speech: طَيِّب.

07كَرِيم
noble, generous
adjectiveك – ر – مmould فَعِيل
الْقُرْآنُ الْكَرِيمُ.

The Noble Qur'an.

Both words definite — so this is a phrase, not a sentence. Chapter 1's trap, and you can now hear it instantly.

08عَظِيم
mighty, tremendous, immense
adjectiveع – ظ – مmould فَعِيل
سُبْحَانَ رَبِّيَ الْعَظِيمِ.

Glory be to my Lord, the Immense.

Said in every rukūʿ of every prayer. Note the adjective takes a kasra — because رَبِّي is in jarr after سُبْحَانَ, and the follower follows.

09قَرِيب
near, close
adjectiveق – ر – بmould فَعِيل
الْمَسْجِدُ قَرِيبٌ مِنْ بَيْتِي.

The mosque is near my house.

10بَعِيد
far, distant
adjectiveب – ع – دmould فَعِيل
طَلَبَ الْعِلْمَ فِي بِلَادٍ بَعِيدَةٍ.

He sought knowledge in distant lands.

11سَهْل
easy
adjectiveس – ه – ل
الدَّرْسُ سَهْلٌ.

The lesson is easy.

12صَعْب
difficult, hard
adjectiveص – ع – ب
لَيْسَ الْأَمْرُ صَعْبًا.

The matter is not difficult.

Note the fatḥa: لَيْسَ throws its predicate into naṣb. Chapter 1 promised you this word; the masterclass explained it; here it is, behaving exactly as advertised.

13حَسَن
good, fine, beautiful
adjectiveح – س – نalso a hadith grade
هٰذَا حَدِيثٌ حَسَنٌ.

This is a ḥasan hadith.

In hadith scholarship it is a technical grade: صَحِيح (authentic) > حَسَن (good) > ضَعِيف (weak). You will hear this triplet in every hadith lecture.

14وَجَدَ
he found
verb · pastو – ج – دassimilated · يَجِدُ
وَجَدْتُ الْكِتَابَ فِي بَيْتِي.

I found the book in my house.

Its maṣdar, وُجُود, is the philosophers' word for existence — and مَوْجُود, "found," is how Arabic says something exists.

15ذَهَبَ
he went, he departed
verb · pastذ – ه – بTop 50
ذَهَبَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ.

The students went to the mosque.

And from it, مَذْهَب — literally a way of going, and therefore a school of legal thought. Ḥanafī, Mālikī, Shāfiʿī, Ḥanbalī: four ways of going.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
الْقُرْآنُ الْكَرِيمُ

The Noble Qur'an. — a phrase, not a sentence.

Both definite. Noun + follower. Nothing is being claimed here; a thing is being named.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْقُرْآنُthe noun — no news arrives, so no sentence · اِسْممَرْفُوع الْكَرِيمُadjective — matches in case, definiteness, gender · نَعْتمَرْفُوع
02
الْقُرْآنُ كَرِيمٌ.

The Qur'an is noble. — a full sentence.

One definite, one not. Now something is being said. This is the whole distinction of Chapter 1, and it lives or dies on a single tanwīn.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْقُرْآنُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع كَرِيمٌthe news — indefinite, so the sentence completes · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
03
هٰذَا حَدِيثٌ صَحِيحٌ.

This is an authentic hadith.

When you hear it: the verdict on a narration. Followed, always, by the source.

Iʿrāb — word by word هٰذَاsubject — frozen pointing word · مُبْتَدَأمَبْنِيّ حَدِيثٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع صَحِيحٌadjective of the news · نَعْتمَرْفُوع
04
لَيْسَ الْأَمْرُ صَعْبًا.

The matter is not difficult.

Watch the fatḥa. لَيْسَ knocks the predicate into naṣb, exactly as كَانَ does.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَيْسَthe flipper — negates, and puts naṣb on its news · فِعْل نَاسِخ— الْأَمْرُits noun · اِسْم لَيْسَمَرْفُوع صَعْبًاits news — flipped to naṣb · خَبَر لَيْسَمَنْصُوب
05
كَلِمَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ صَدَقَةٌ.

A good word is a charity.

A hadith. Also a perfect specimen: كَلِمَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ is noun + adjective (both indefinite, so they bind together), and صَدَقَةٌ is the predicate. Subject phrase, then the claim.

Iʿrāb — word by word كَلِمَةٌsubject — indefinite, but rescued by its adjective · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع طَيِّبَةٌits adjective · نَعْتمَرْفُوع صَدَقَةٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
06
الْمَسْجِدُ قَرِيبٌ مِنْ بَيْتِي.

The mosque is near my house.

Everything from Chapter 4 is here: a preposition, a noun, an attached pronoun — and the case ending vanishing beneath the ـِي.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْمَسْجِدُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع قَرِيبٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع مِنْpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— بَيْتِيafter the preposition + my · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
07
وَجَدْتُ كِتَابًا قَدِيمًا.

I found an old book.

Both in naṣb. The noun is the object, so it takes a fatḥa — and the adjective follows it into the fatḥa. That is the fourth agreement: case.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَجَدْتُverb + I · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل— كِتَابًاobject · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب قَدِيمًاadjective — follows its noun into naṣb · نَعْتمَنْصُوب
08
الْكُتُبُ الْقَدِيمَةُ كَثِيرَةٌ.

The old books are many.

The strange one. كُتُب is a plural — but books are not people, so Arabic treats them as feminine singular. Hence الْقَدِيمَةُ, not al-qadīmūn. Section 4 explains.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْكُتُبُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع الْقَدِيمَةُadjective — feminine singular, because broken plurals of things take it · نَعْتمَرْفُوع كَثِيرَةٌthe news — feminine singular again · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
09
ذَهَبَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْكَبِيرِ.

The students went to the big mosque.

Hear the two kasras. The noun is in jarr after إِلَى — and the adjective follows it down. Wherever the noun goes, the follower goes.

Iʿrāb — word by word ذَهَبَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الطُّلَّابُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع إِلَىpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْمَسْجِدِafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور الْكَبِيرِadjective — follows its noun into jarr · نَعْتمَجْرُور
10
الْعِلْمُ سَهْلٌ، وَلٰكِنَّ الْعَمَلَ صَعْبٌ.

Knowledge is easy — but action is difficult.

Notice وَلٰكِنَّ: a sister of إِنَّ, and it does the same thing — it throws the following noun into naṣb. That fatḥa on الْعَمَلَ is not an accident.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْعِلْمُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع سَهْلٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع وَلٰكِنَّand + but — an إِنَّ-sister, naṣb on its noun · حَرْف اِسْتِدْرَاك— الْعَمَلَits noun · اِسْم لٰكِنَّمَنْصُوب صَعْبٌits news · خَبَر لٰكِنَّمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — The Adjective Follows in Four Ways

النَّعْت — literally, the follower.

An English adjective is lazy. Big is big whether the house is one or ten, near or far, the subject or the object. Arabic will not permit that. The adjective must match its noun in four separate respects, simultaneously.

Must match inExample
1. Definiteness
both take الـ, or neither does		الْبَيْتُ الْكَبِيرُ · بَيْتٌ كَبِيرٌ
2. Genderكِتَابٌ كَبِيرٌ · مَدْرَسَةٌ كَبِيرَةٌ
3. Numberطَالِبٌ صَالِحٌ · طُلَّابٌ صَالِحُونَ
4. Case
rafʿ / naṣb / jarr		فِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْكَبِيرِ — both in jarr
Why this is a gift, not a burden

Four agreements sound like four chances to fail. But for a listener, they are four chances to succeed. When a speaker says فِي الْمَسْجِدِ الْكَبِيرِ, that second kasra tells you — before he has even finished the phrase — that الْكَبِيرِ belongs to الْمَسْجِدِ and not to some other noun floating nearby.

Arabic is redundant on purpose. Redundancy is what lets you understand speech through noise, at speed, without a script. Every ending you learn to hear is another handrail.

The distinction that decides everything

Return to it one more time, because it is the hinge of the whole language:

الْبَيْتُ الْكَبِيرُ

the big house — a phrase. Both definite. Nothing has been claimed.

الْبَيْتُ كَبِيرٌ.

The house is big. — a sentence. One definite, one not. A claim has been made.

Same two words. Same order. The tanwīn is the entire difference between naming a thing and saying something about it. This is why the vowels are not decoration and never were.

The rule that will feel wrong for a month

Arabic divides plurals into two kinds, and treats them differently:

Plural of...Treated asExample
Peoplea real pluralالطُّلَّابُ الصَّالِحُونَ — the righteous students
Non-people
things, animals, ideas		feminine singularالْكُتُبُ الْقَدِيمَةُ — the old books
Read that again

"The old books" is, to Arabic, the old book-collection — grammatically she. So the adjective is feminine singular, and so is the verb:

الْكُتُبُ الْقَدِيمَةُ مَوْجُودَةٌ فِي الْمَكْتَبَةِ. — "The old books are present in the library."

Every learner finds this bizarre, and every learner stops noticing it within a few weeks. Do not fight it. Just start expecting a ة whenever the subject is a pile of things.

The فَعِيل mould — where the adjectives live

Look at today's list: كَبِير · صَغِير · جَدِيد · قَدِيم · كَرِيم · عَظِيم · قَرِيب · بَعِيد. Eight adjectives, one shape. faʿīl.

This mould is an adjective factory, and it produces most of the great religious vocabulary — including a large share of the Names of Allah:

الْعَلِيم · السَّمِيع · الْحَكِيم · الْكَرِيم · الْعَظِيم · الْقَدِير · الْحَفِيظ

The Knowing · The Hearing · The Wise · The Noble · The Immense · The Capable · The Preserver

Every one of them is a root you now know or will soon know, poured into فَعِيل. عَلِمَ → عَلِيم. سَمِعَ → سَمِيع. حَفِظَ → حَفِيظ. The mould does not merely mean "one who does" — it means one who does it intensely, permanently, by nature. That intensity is precisely why it is the mould of the divine names.

Section 5Reading Practice

الْكِتَابُ الْقَدِيمُ

ذَهَبْتُ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْكَبِيرِ، وَهُوَ قَرِيبٌ مِنْ بَيْتِي.

وَجَدْتُ فِي الْمَسْجِدِ كِتَابًا قَدِيمًا. الْكِتَابُ صَغِيرٌ، وَلٰكِنَّ عِلْمَهُ كَثِيرٌ.

جَلَسْتُ وَقَرَأْتُ. كَانَتِ الْكَلِمَاتُ صَعْبَةً، وَلٰكِنَّ الْمَعْنَىٰ كَانَ جَمِيلًا.

قَالَ الْكَاتِبُ: الْعِلْمُ سَهْلٌ، وَالْعَمَلُ صَعْبٌ. وَالْعَالِمُ الصَّالِحُ هُوَ الَّذِي يَعْمَلُ بِعِلْمِهِ.

رَجَعْتُ إِلَى بَيْتِي، وَفِي قَلْبِي كَلِمَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ.

The Old Book

I went to the big mosque, which is near my house.

I found an old book in the mosque. The book is small, but its knowledge is great.

I sat and read. The words were difficult, but the meaning was beautiful.

The writer said: knowledge is easy, and action is difficult. And the righteous scholar is the one who acts by his knowledge.

I returned to my house, and in my heart was a good word.

New in context: جَمِيل beautiful · الْمَعْنَىٰ the meaning · الْكَاتِب the writer · بِعِلْمِهِ by his knowledge

Two things worth catching

كَانَتِ الْكَلِمَاتُ صَعْبَةً — "the words were difficult." كَلِمَات is a non-human plural, so the verb is feminine singular (كَانَتْ) and so is the adjective (صَعْبَةً). And that adjective is in naṣb, because كَانَ demands it. Three rules, one phrase.

الْعَالِمُ الصَّالِحُ هُوَ الَّذِي يَعْمَلُ — and there is the pronoun of separation from the masterclass, doing its job: both halves are definite, so هُوَ steps in to say this is a sentence, not a phrase.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

يَقُولُ النَّاسُ: اللُّغَةُ الْعَرَبِيَّةُ لُغَةٌ صَعْبَةٌ.

وَأَنَا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ: لَيْسَتْ صَعْبَةً. هِيَ لُغَةٌ عَظِيمَةٌ، وَلٰكِنَّهَا لَيْسَتْ صَعْبَةً.

الْكَلِمَاتُ الْجَدِيدَةُ كَثِيرَةٌ، نَعَمْ. وَلٰكِنَّ الْقَوَاعِدَ قَلِيلَةٌ.

اِعْلَمُوا: الطَّالِبُ الصَّالِحُ هُوَ الَّذِي يَدْرُسُ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ، قَلِيلًا قَلِيلًا.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَكَمْ كَلِمَةً أَحْفَظُ فِي الْيَوْمِ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

خَمْسَ عَشْرَةَ كَلِمَةً. لَا أَكْثَرَ. وَلٰكِنْ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ.

Shaykh: People say: the Arabic language is a difficult language. And I say to you: it is not difficult. It is a mighty language — but it is not difficult. The new words are many, yes. But the rules are few. Know this: the good student is the one who studies every day, little by little.

Student: Shaykh, and how many words should I memorise in a day?

Shaykh: Fifteen words. No more. But every single day.

New: لَيْسَتْ she/it is not · الْقَوَاعِد the rules · قَلِيل few · كَمْ how many · خَمْسَ عَشْرَةَ fifteen · أَكْثَر more

Say it yourself

اللُّغَةُ الْعَرَبِيَّةُ لَيْسَتْ صَعْبَةً.

وَجَدْتُ كِتَابًا قَدِيمًا.

الْمَسْجِدُ قَرِيبٌ مِنْ بَيْتِي.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb وَجَدَ

An assimilated verb — it opens with و, and that و simply evaporates in the present: وَجَدَ but يَجِدُ, not yawjidu. The same thing happens to وَصَلَ → يَصِلُ (to arrive) and وَعَدَ → يَعِدُ (to promise). One rule, a whole family of verbs. Its maṣdar, وُجُود, is the philosophers' word for existence.

maṣdar وُجُود existence, finding
to find
Form I · assimilated (و) · يَجِدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIوَجَدْتُأَجِدُ——
نَحْنُweوَجَدْنَانَجِدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)وَجَدْتَتَجِدُجِدْلَا تَجِدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)وَجَدْتِتَجِدِينَجِدِيلَا تَجِدِي
هُوَheوَجَدَيَجِدُ——
هِيَsheوَجَدَتْتَجِدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)وَجَدْتُمَاتَجِدَانِجِدَالَا تَجِدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)وَجَدْتُمْتَجِدُونَجِدُوالَا تَجِدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)وَجَدْتُمَاتَجِدَانِجِدَالَا تَجِدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)وَجَدْتُنَّتَجِدْنَجِدْنَلَا تَجِدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)وَجَدَايَجِدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)وَجَدُوايَجِدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)وَجَدَتَاتَجِدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)وَجَدْنَيَجِدْنَ——
verb ذَهَبَ
maṣdar ذَهَاب going
to go, to depart
Form I · sound · يَذْهَبُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIذَهَبْتُأَذْهَبُ——
نَحْنُweذَهَبْنَانَذْهَبُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)ذَهَبْتَتَذْهَبُاِذْهَبْلَا تَذْهَبْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)ذَهَبْتِتَذْهَبِينَاِذْهَبِيلَا تَذْهَبِي
هُوَheذَهَبَيَذْهَبُ——
هِيَsheذَهَبَتْتَذْهَبُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)ذَهَبْتُمَاتَذْهَبَانِاِذْهَبَالَا تَذْهَبَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)ذَهَبْتُمْتَذْهَبُونَاِذْهَبُوالَا تَذْهَبُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)ذَهَبْتُمَاتَذْهَبَانِاِذْهَبَالَا تَذْهَبَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)ذَهَبْتُنَّتَذْهَبْنَاِذْهَبْنَلَا تَذْهَبْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)ذَهَبَايَذْهَبَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)ذَهَبُوايَذْهَبُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)ذَهَبَتَاتَذْهَبَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)ذَهَبْنَيَذْهَبْنَ——

أَيْنَ ذَهَبَ؟ — “Where did he go?” Also the root of مَذْهَب: literally a way of going, and hence a school of law.

The adjectives — and their mould

فَعِيلthe adjective factory
Today's: كَبِير · صَغِير · جَدِيد · قَدِيم · كَرِيم · عَظِيم · قَرِيب · بَعِيد · حَسَن
Feminine: add ة — كَبِيرَة · جَدِيدَة · عَظِيمَة
Comparative: the mould أَفْعَل — أَكْبَر bigger · أَصْغَر smaller · أَقْرَب nearer · أَبْعَد farther
One shape in, one shape out. You now generate comparatives for every adjective you meet — including ones you have never seen.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · active المضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itself اسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passive المضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-to الأمرcommand
to findوَجَدَيَجِدُوُجُودوَاجِدوُجِدَيُوجَدُمَوْجُودجِدْ
to goذَهَبَيَذْهَبُذَهَابذَاهِب———اِذْهَبْ
One root through all eight moulds — وَجَدَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root و ج د, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. وَجَدَ الْحَقَّ بَعْدَ طُولِ بَحْثٍ. — He found the truth after a long search.

المضارع · present active. يَجِدُ الصَّابِرُ ثَمَرَةَ صَبْرِهِ. — The patient one finds the fruit of his patience.

المصدر · the action itself. وُجُودُ اللهِ ثَابِتٌ بِالْعَقْلِ وَالنَّقْلِ. — The existence of Allah is established by reason and revelation.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْوَاجِدُ لِلْحَقِّ لَا يَتْرُكُهُ. — One who has found the truth does not abandon it.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. وُجِدَ الْكِتَابُ فِي الْمَسْجِدِ. — The book was found in the mosque.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. لَا يُوجَدُ عِلْمٌ بِلَا تَعَبٍ. — No knowledge is found without effort.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. اللهُ مَوْجُودٌ لَا كَالْمَوْجُودَاتِ. — Allah exists, unlike [other] existing things.

الأمر · command. جِدْ لِنَفْسِكَ صَاحِبًا صَالِحًا. — Find yourself a righteous companion.

The word for existence

وَجَدَ means "he found." Its maṣdar is وُجُود. Its done-to noun is مَوْجُود.

And in Arabic, to say a thing exists, you say it is مَوْجُود — found. Existence, in this language, is not an abstract property a thing possesses. It is the condition of being findable. Ibn Sīnā and al-Ghazālī built centuries of metaphysics on top of that one word, and it was sitting inside a verb you learned today in thirty seconds.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

كَبِيرbig, great صَغِيرsmall, young جَدِيدnew قَدِيمold, ancient صَالِحrighteous, sound طَيِّبgood, pure كَرِيمnoble, generous عَظِيمmighty, immense قَرِيبnear بَعِيدfar سَهْلeasy صَعْبdifficult حَسَنgood, fine وَجَدَhe found ذَهَبَhe went

Spiral recap — Chapters 2 & 3

جَعَلَhe made / placed أَخَذَhe took دَخَلَhe entered خَرَجَhe went out فَهِمَhe understood طَلَبَhe sought حَفِظَhe memorised رَجَعَhe returned الْمَسْجِدُthe mosque دَائِمًاalways

Grammar recap — everything so far, on one page

ChapterStructureThe tell
1Nominal sentenceTwo nouns, no verb. Definite + indefinite.
★Iʿrābḍamma = doer · fatḥa = object · kasra = after a preposition or possessed
2Verbal sentence, pastVerb first. Doer in rafʿ, object in naṣb.
3Present tenseThe prefixes أ · ن · ي · ت
4Iḍāfa & attached pronounsNo الـ on the first noun; kasra on the second
5The adjectiveFollows in definiteness, gender, number, case
Before you close the book

Seventy-five words, and the entire skeleton of Arabic syntax. You can now name every ending in بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ, explain why الرَّحْمٰنِ is in jarr, and say why الْحَمْدُ is not.

What remains is not harder. It is only more. Grammar is a small country and you have already walked most of it. Vocabulary is the ocean — and that is a matter of days, not of difficulty.

End of Chapter 5 Next → The Sisters of إنّ and كان
Day 06 · Grammar: the sisters of كان and إنّ

The Words That
Bend a Sentence

Two small families of words exist for one purpose only: to walk into a perfectly good sentence and rearrange its endings. Learn them, and half of your remaining "why is that vowel like that?" questions vanish at once.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Master كَانَ and its sisters — the verbs that knock the predicate into naṣb.
  2. Master إِنَّ and its sisters — the particles that do the exact opposite.
  3. Hear إِنَّ اللهَ and know, instantly, why that fatḥa is there.
  4. Meet your first derived verb form — أَصْبَحَ — and learn the one sound that gives every derived form away.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01إِنَّ
indeed, verily, truly
particlethrows its noun into naṣbTop 10
إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ.

Indeed, Allah is with the patient.

Often untranslatable — it simply adds weight. English reaches for "indeed" or "truly," but the honest translation is often nothing at all: it is emphasis you hear, not emphasis you read.

02أَنَّ
that (introducing a clause)
particlesister of إِنَّ
عَلِمْتُ أَنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

I knew that knowledge is light.

The difference from إِنَّ is purely positional: إِنَّ opens a sentence; أَنَّ hangs a sentence off a verb. Both force naṣb.

03لٰكِنَّ
but, however
particlesister of إِنَّ
الْعِلْمُ سَهْلٌ، وَلٰكِنَّ الْعَمَلَ صَعْبٌ.

Knowledge is easy, but action is difficult.

Careful: لٰكِنَّ (with shadda) takes a noun and forces naṣb. لٰكِنْ (without) is a simple "but" and changes nothing. Same word, two weights.

04لَعَلَّ
perhaps, so that, in the hope that
particlesister of إِنَّ
لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ.

Perhaps you will reason. / So that you may understand.

A Qur'anic refrain — لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ, لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ. The pronoun ـكُمْ attached to it is in naṣb, exactly as the rule demands.

05كَأَنَّ
as if, as though
particlesister of إِنَّ
كَأَنَّ الْقَلْبَ مِرْآةٌ.

As if the heart were a mirror.

06صَارَ
he/it became
verb · hollowص – ي – رsister of كَانَ
صَارَ الطَّالِبُ عَالِمًا.

The student became a scholar.

Note the fatḥa on عَالِمًا. That is the sister of كَانَ doing its work.

07أَصْبَحَ
he/it became; he entered the morning
verb · Form IVص – ب – حsister of كَانَ
أَصْبَحَ الْأَمْرُ وَاضِحًا.

The matter became clear.

Literally "he entered the morning" — and by extension, "he came to be." Arabic has a whole family of these: أَمْسَىٰ (entered the evening), أَضْحَىٰ (entered the forenoon). All of them mean became, with a time of day quietly attached.

08مَا زَالَ
he/it still is; he has not ceased
verb phrasesister of كَانَ
مَا زَالَ الْعِلْمُ نُورًا.

Knowledge is still a light.

Literally "it has not ceased [to be]." A double negative that Arabic uses as a plain positive.

09صَبَرَ
he was patient, he endured
verb · pastص – ب – رmaṣdar صَبْر
إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ.

Indeed, Allah is with the patient.

10الْإِيمَانُ
faith, belief
noun · maṣdarأ – م – نVery high
الْإِيمَانُ فِي الْقَلْبِ.

Faith is in the heart.

Same root as أَمْن (safety) and أَمَانَة (trust). To believe, in Arabic, is to render safe — to place your trust somewhere. And مُؤْمِن, a believer, is one who does so.

11الصَّبْرُ
patience, endurance, steadfastness
noun · maṣdarص – ب – ر
الصَّبْرُ مِفْتَاحُ الْفَرَجِ.

Patience is the key to relief.

A proverb — and a nominal sentence with an iḍāfa as its predicate. Everything you learned in Chapters 1 and 4, in five words.

12الْحَيَاةُ
life
noun · fem.ح – ي – ي
الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا قَصِيرَةٌ.

The life of this world is short.

الْحَيَاة الدُّنْيَا — "the nearest life" — is the standard Qur'anic phrase for this world, as opposed to الْآخِرَة, the last.

13الْمَوْتُ
death
noun · maṣdarم – و – ت
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ.

Every soul shall taste death.

14الْخَيْرُ
good, goodness; better
nounخ – ي – رVery high
الْخَيْرُ كَثِيرٌ فِي هٰذِهِ الْأُمَّةِ.

There is much good in this community.

15الشَّرُّ
evil, harm
nounش – ر – ر
الشَّرُّ قَلِيلٌ، وَالْخَيْرُ كَثِيرٌ.

Evil is little, and good is much.

Learn خَيْر and شَرّ as a pair. Both also serve as comparatives: خَيْرٌ مِنْ = "better than," شَرٌّ مِنْ = "worse than" — irregular comparatives that skip the أَفْعَل mould entirely.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ.

Indeed, Allah is with the patient.

The fatḥa on اللهَ is the whole lesson of this chapter. You have heard this sentence a thousand times. Today you know why it sounds the way it does.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis — naṣb on its noun · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— اللهَits noun · اِسْم إِنَّمَنْصُوب مَعَadverb "with" — the news, possessing the next · ظَرْف · خَبَر · مُضَافمَنْصُوب الصَّابِرِينَpossessed — īna for jarr · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
02
إِنَّ الْحَمْدَ لِـلهِ.

Indeed, all praise is for Allah.

Compare with Day 4: الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ — ḍamma. Add إِنَّ and it becomes الْحَمْدَ — fatḥa. The opening of the khuṭbat al-ḥājah, delivered before nearly every sermon.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— الْحَمْدَits noun · اِسْم إِنَّمَنْصُوب لِلهِpreposition + jarr — the news · خَبَر · شِبْه جُمْلَةمَجْرُور
03
كَانَ الرَّسُولُ ﷺ رَحْمَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ.

The Messenger ﷺ was a mercy to the worlds.

The mirror image. Subject stays in rafʿ; predicate drops to naṣb.

Iʿrāb — word by word كَانَthe flipper — rafʿ on its noun, naṣb on its news · فِعْل نَاسِخ— الرَّسُولُits noun · اِسْم كَانَمَرْفُوع رَحْمَةًits news · خَبَر كَانَمَنْصُوب لِلْعَالَمِينَpreposition + jarr · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور
04
أَصْبَحَ الْأَمْرُ وَاضِحًا.

The matter became clear.

When you hear it: a speaker announcing that his argument has landed.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَصْبَحَ"became" — a كَانَ-sister · فِعْل نَاسِخ— الْأَمْرُits noun · اِسْم أَصْبَحَمَرْفُوع وَاضِحًاits news · خَبَر أَصْبَحَمَنْصُوب
05
عَلِمْتُ أَنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

I knew that knowledge is light.

The hinge of every argument. أَنَّ lets a speaker stack one full statement inside another — which is what reasoning is.

Iʿrāb — word by word عَلِمْتُverb + I · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل— أَنَّ"that" — إِنَّ's sister · حَرْف تَوْكِيد وَنَصْب— الْعِلْمَits noun · اِسْم أَنَّمَنْصُوب نُورٌits news · خَبَر أَنَّمَرْفُوع
06
الصَّبْرُ مِفْتَاحُ الْفَرَجِ.

Patience is the key to relief.

A proverb. Nominal sentence; the predicate is an iḍāfa.

Iʿrāb — word by word الصَّبْرُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع مِفْتَاحُthe news, possessing the next · خَبَر · مُضَافمَرْفُوع الْفَرَجِpossessed · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور
07
مَا زَالَ الْعِلْمُ نُورًا.

Knowledge is still a light.

Notice the naṣb. مَا زَالَ is a sister of كَانَ and behaves exactly like her.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَا زَالَ"is still" — a كَانَ-sister · فِعْل نَاسِخ— الْعِلْمُits noun · اِسْم مَا زَالَمَرْفُوع نُورًاits news · خَبَرُهَامَنْصُوب
08
لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ.

Perhaps you will reason.

Qur'anic refrain. The ـكُمْ is the noun of لَعَلَّ, and it is in naṣb — you just cannot hear it, because attached pronouns hide their case.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَعَلَّكُمْ"perhaps" + you — an إِنَّ-sister with its noun attached · حَرْف تَرَجٍّ + اِسْمُهَا— تَعْقِلُونَverb, present — the whole clause is the news · فِعْل مُضَارِع · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
09
الْعِلْمُ خَيْرٌ مِنَ الْمَالِ.

Knowledge is better than wealth.

Notice: خَيْر is doing the work of a comparative with no أَفْعَل mould in sight. It is irregular, and it is everywhere.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْعِلْمُsubject · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع خَيْرٌ"better" — the news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع مِنَthe preposition of comparison · حَرْف جَرّ— الْمَالِafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
10
لَيْسَ الْخَبَرُ كَـالْمُعَايَنَةِ.

Hearing about a thing is not like seeing it.

A hadith, and a proverb. لَيْسَ — the great sister of كَانَ — is Arabic's only verb that means "is not."

Iʿrāb — word by word لَيْسَthe negating flipper · فِعْل نَاسِخ— الْخَبَرُits noun · اِسْم لَيْسَمَرْفُوع كَالْمُعَايَنَةِlike + jarr — the news · خَبَر · شِبْه جُمْلَةمَجْرُور

Section 4Grammar — Two Families, Two Directions

Take a plain nominal sentence. Both halves in rafʿ:

الْعِلْمُ نُورٌ.

Knowledge is light. (-u ... -un)

Now put a word in front of it. Two families exist, and they push in opposite directions.

Family one: كَانَ and her sisters

These are verbs. They leave the subject alone and knock the predicate down into naṣb.

كَانَ الْعِلْمُ نُورًا.

Knowledge was a light. (subject -u · predicate -an)

SisterMeaning
كَانَwas
لَيْسَis not
صَارَbecame
أَصْبَحَbecame (lit. entered the morning)
مَا زَالَis still, has not ceased
مَا دَامَas long as

Family two: إِنَّ and her sisters

These are particles. They do the exact opposite: they grab the subject and pull it down into naṣb, leaving the predicate in rafʿ.

إِنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

Indeed, knowledge is light. (subject -a · predicate -un)

SisterMeaning
إِنَّindeed, verily
أَنَّthat
لٰكِنَّbut
لَعَلَّperhaps, so that
كَأَنَّas if
لَيْتَif only, would that
The whole chapter, in one line

كَانَ lowers the second. إِنَّ lowers the first.

That is it. Two families, two directions, and between them they account for an enormous share of the fatḥas you hear in formal Arabic.

Why the ear cares

When a khaṭīb says إِنَّ الْحَمْدَ لِلهِ — with a fatḥa on a word you have only ever heard with a ḍamma — that is not a mistake, and it is not a variant. It is إِنَّ, doing precisely what it always does. Every time you correctly predict one of these endings before it arrives, your comprehension gets a little faster, because you are no longer processing the sentence. You are anticipating it.

And a first look at the derived forms

أَصْبَحَ is not a plain verb. It is a Form IV verb — the first derived form you have met. Arabic takes a root and pours it into ten different verb-moulds, each with its own flavour of meaning. You will meet them properly in Level 2, but learn the tell now:

يَكْتُبُ  ·  يُصْبِحُ

Form I: prefix carries a fatḥa — ya-.   Derived: prefix carries a ḍamma — yu-.

That single vowel on the prefix — ya- versus yu- — is your instant signal that you are hearing a derived form. Train your ear for it now, and Level 2 will feel like recognition rather than learning.

Section 5Reading Practice

الصَّبْرُ

كَانَ الطَّالِبُ صَغِيرًا، ثُمَّ صَارَ كَبِيرًا. كَانَ لَا يَعْرِفُ شَيْئًا، ثُمَّ أَصْبَحَ عَالِمًا.

وَكَيْفَ؟ بِالصَّبْرِ. إِنَّ الصَّبْرَ مِفْتَاحُ الْفَرَجِ.

قَالَ لَهُ شَيْخُهُ: يَا بُنَيَّ، الْعِلْمُ لَا يَأْتِي فِي يَوْمٍ وَاحِدٍ. اِصْبِرْ، وَادْرُسْ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ قَلِيلًا.

وَاعْلَمْ أَنَّ الْحَيَاةَ قَصِيرَةٌ، وَأَنَّ الْعِلْمَ طَوِيلٌ. وَلٰكِنَّ الصَّابِرَ يَصِلُ.

فَصَبَرَ الطَّالِبُ سِنِينَ، وَمَا زَالَ يَدْرُسُ حَتَّىٰ أَصْبَحَ إِمَامًا.

Patience

The student was young, then he became old. He used to know nothing, then he became a scholar.

And how? Through patience. Indeed, patience is the key to relief.

His shaykh said to him: my son, knowledge does not come in a single day. Be patient, and study a little every day.

And know that life is short, and that knowledge is long. But the patient one arrives.

So the student was patient for years, and he kept studying until he became an imam.

New in context: كَيْفَ how · مِفْتَاح key · الْفَرَج relief · طَوِيل long · يَصِلُ he arrives · سِنِين years · حَتَّىٰ until · إِمَام imam

Read the endings, not the words

كَانَ الطَّالِبُ صَغِيرًا — subject rafʿ, predicate naṣb. إِنَّ الصَّبْرَ مِفْتَاحُ... — subject naṣb, predicate rafʿ. وَلٰكِنَّ الصَّابِرَ يَصِلُ — naṣb again.

Three sentences, three different endings on the subject, and every one of them predicted by a single word standing at the front. That is what it means to hear grammar.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

إِنَّ الْحَمْدَ لِلهِ، نَحْمَدُهُ وَنَسْتَعِينُهُ.

أَيُّهَا الْإِخْوَةُ، إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ. هٰذِهِ كَلِمَةٌ قَصِيرَةٌ، وَلٰكِنَّ مَعْنَاهَا عَظِيمٌ.

كَانَ النَّاسُ فِي الْأَوَّلِ قَلِيلِينَ، ثُمَّ صَارُوا كَثِيرِينَ. وَمَا صَارُوا كَثِيرِينَ إِلَّا بِالصَّبْرِ.

فَاصْبِرْ، يَا أَخِي. إِنَّ الْحَيَاةَ قَصِيرَةٌ، وَالْأَجْرَ عَظِيمٌ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، لِمَاذَا تَقُولُ «إِنَّ الْحَمْدَ» وَلَا تَقُولُ «الْحَمْدُ»؟

الشَّيْخُ:

سُؤَالٌ جَمِيلٌ. «إِنَّ» تَنْصِبُ الِاسْمَ. هٰذِهِ قَاعِدَةٌ، وَلَيْسَتْ صَعْبَةً.

Shaykh: Indeed, all praise is for Allah; we praise Him and seek His help. Brothers — indeed Allah is with the patient. This is a short phrase, but its meaning is immense. The people were few at first, then they became many. And they became many only through patience. So be patient, my brother. Life is short, and the reward is immense.

Student: Shaykh, why do you say inna l-ḥamda and not al-ḥamdu?

Shaykh: A beautiful question. إِنَّ puts the noun into naṣb. That is a rule — and it is not a difficult one.

New: الْإِخْوَة the brothers · فِي الْأَوَّلِ at first · إِلَّا except, only · الْأَجْر the reward · لِمَاذَا why · تَنْصِبُ it puts into naṣb · قَاعِدَة a rule

Say it yourself

إِنَّ الْعِلْمَ نُورٌ.

كَانَ الدَّرْسُ سَهْلًا.

أَصْبَحَ الْأَمْرُ وَاضِحًا.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb صَبَرَ
maṣdar صَبْر patience, endurance
to be patient, to endure
Form I · sound · يَصْبِرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIصَبَرْتُأَصْبِرُ——
نَحْنُweصَبَرْنَانَصْبِرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)صَبَرْتَتَصْبِرُاِصْبِرْلَا تَصْبِرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)صَبَرْتِتَصْبِرِينَاِصْبِرِيلَا تَصْبِرِي
هُوَheصَبَرَيَصْبِرُ——
هِيَsheصَبَرَتْتَصْبِرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)صَبَرْتُمَاتَصْبِرَانِاِصْبِرَالَا تَصْبِرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)صَبَرْتُمْتَصْبِرُونَاِصْبِرُوالَا تَصْبِرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)صَبَرْتُمَاتَصْبِرَانِاِصْبِرَالَا تَصْبِرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)صَبَرْتُنَّتَصْبِرْنَاِصْبِرْنَلَا تَصْبِرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)صَبَرَايَصْبِرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)صَبَرُوايَصْبِرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)صَبَرَتَاتَصْبِرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)صَبَرْنَيَصْبِرْنَ——

اِصْبِرْ — “be patient.” The command form is one of the most repeated imperatives in the Qur'an. And الصَّابِرُون, “the patient ones,” is simply the doer-mould in the plural.

verb صَارَ
maṣdar صَيْرُورَة becoming
to become
Form I · hollow (ي) · يَصِيرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIصِرْتُأَصِيرُ——
نَحْنُweصِرْنَانَصِيرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)صِرْتَتَصِيرُصِرْلَا تَصِرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)صِرْتِتَصِيرِينَصِيرِيلَا تَصِيرِي
هُوَheصَارَيَصِيرُ——
هِيَsheصَارَتْتَصِيرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)صِرْتُمَاتَصِيرَانِصِيرَالَا تَصِيرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)صِرْتُمْتَصِيرُونَصِيرُوالَا تَصِيرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)صِرْتُمَاتَصِيرَانِصِيرَالَا تَصِيرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)صِرْتُنَّتَصِرْنَصِرْنَلَا تَصِرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)صَارَايَصِيرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)صَارُوايَصِيرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)صَارَتَاتَصِيرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)صِرْنَيَصِرْنَ——

The same hollow machinery as قَالَ and كَانَ — but built on a ي instead of a و, so the long vowel in the present is ī, not ū. ṣāra → yaṣīru, exactly as kāna → yakūnu. One pattern, two flavours.

verb أَصْبَحَ
maṣdar إِصْبَاح becoming, dawning
to become; to enter the morning
Form IV · يُصْبِحُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIأَصْبَحْتُأُصْبِحُ——
نَحْنُweأَصْبَحْنَانُصْبِحُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)أَصْبَحْتَتُصْبِحُأَصْبِحْلَا تُصْبِحْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)أَصْبَحْتِتُصْبِحِينَأَصْبِحِيلَا تُصْبِحِي
هُوَheأَصْبَحَيُصْبِحُ——
هِيَsheأَصْبَحَتْتُصْبِحُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)أَصْبَحْتُمَاتُصْبِحَانِأَصْبِحَالَا تُصْبِحَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)أَصْبَحْتُمْتُصْبِحُونَأَصْبِحُوالَا تُصْبِحُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)أَصْبَحْتُمَاتُصْبِحَانِأَصْبِحَالَا تُصْبِحَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)أَصْبَحْتُنَّتُصْبِحْنَأَصْبِحْنَلَا تُصْبِحْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)أَصْبَحَايُصْبِحَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)أَصْبَحُوايُصْبِحُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)أَصْبَحَتَاتُصْبِحَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)أَصْبَحْنَيُصْبِحْنَ——

Your first Form IV verb — and the doorway to the whole system of derived forms. Notice the tell: the present tense begins with u-, not a-. يُصْبِحُ, not yaṣbaḥu. Whenever you hear a present-tense verb whose prefix carries a ḍamma, you are hearing a derived form — and that single sound is worth training your ear on now.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to be patientصَبَرَيَصْبِرُصَبْرصَابِر———اِصْبِرْ
to becomeصَارَيَصِيرُصَيْرُورَةصَائِر———صِرْ
to become (IV)أَصْبَحَيُصْبِحُإِصْبَاحمُصْبِح———أَصْبِحْ
One root through all eight moulds — صَبَرَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ص ب ر, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. صَبَرَ عَلَى الْبَلَاءِ. — He was patient through the trial.

المضارع · present active. يَصْبِرُ الْمُؤْمِنُ وَلَا يَيْأَسُ. — The believer is patient and does not despair.

المصدر · the action itself. الصَّبْرُ ضِيَاءٌ. — Patience is a light.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. اللهُ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ. — Allah is with the patient.

الماضي المجهول · المضارع المجهول · اسم المفعول. No passive. صَبَرَ takes no object — there is nothing to make the subject of a passive — so the three cells stay empty. The gap in that row is the rule, not a missing word.

الأمر · command. اِصْبِرْ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ. — Be patient — indeed Allah’s promise is true.

Notice the gaps

صَبَرَ · صَارَ · أَصْبَحَ — all three have empty passive cells. None of them takes an object, and a verb with no object can have no passive. The holes are the rule speaking.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

إِنَّindeed أَنَّthat لٰكِنَّbut لَعَلَّperhaps كَأَنَّas if صَارَhe became أَصْبَحَhe became مَا زَالَhe still is صَبَرَhe was patient الْإِيمَانُfaith الصَّبْرُpatience الْحَيَاةُlife الْمَوْتُdeath الْخَيْرُgood الشَّرُّevil

Spiral recap — Chapters 3 & 4

سَأَلَhe asked عَمِلَhe worked / acted جَلَسَhe sat الرَّبُّthe Lord الْعَبْدُthe servant الْقَلْبُthe heart الدِّينُthe religion أَهْلُpeople of
Before you close the book

Ninety words. And you have just closed the loop the masterclass opened: every fatḥa you were told to expect after إِنَّ, you can now not only expect but explain. That is the difference between a learner who has been told a rule and one who owns it.

End of Chapter 6 Next → Saying No, and Asking Why
Day 07 · Grammar: negation & the question words

Saying No,
and Asking Why

Arabic has four ways to say "not," and it chooses between them by time. English has one word and a helper verb. Arabic has four words and no helper at all — which sounds harder, and is in fact far easier, once you see the logic.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Learn all four negation particles — لَا · مَا · لَمْ · لَنْ — and know exactly which tense each one attacks.
  2. Learn the nine question words, and hear how a question is built with no change in word order at all.
  3. Understand why لَمْ يَفْعَلْ uses a present-tense verb to mean a past thing.
  4. Meet عَرَفَ and قَدَرَ, and the theology hiding inside مَعْرُوف and قَدَر.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01لَا
not (present); no; do not!
particlenegates the present
لَا أَفْهَمُ هٰذَا.

I do not understand this.

02مَا
not (past); also: what?
particletwo jobs
مَا قَالَ شَيْئًا.

He did not say anything.

Context alone separates the two uses: مَا فَعَلَ؟ is “what did he do?” and مَا فَعَلَ. is “he did not do [it].” Arabs hear the difference in the intonation, exactly as you do in English.

03لَمْ
did not
particlepresent shape, past meaningcauses jazm
لَمْ يَفْهَمِ الطَّالِبُ الدَّرْسَ.

The student did not understand the lesson.

04لَنْ
will not (ever)
particlecauses naṣb
لَنْ أَنْسَىٰ هٰذَا الْيَوْمَ.

I will never forget this day.

Stronger than a simple future negative — it carries finality. Never.

05مَنْ
who?
question word
مَنْ رَوَىٰ هٰذَا الْحَدِيثَ؟

Who narrated this hadith?

06مَاذَا
what?
question word
مَاذَا يَعْنِي هٰذَا؟

What does this mean?

07مَتَىٰ
when?
question word
مَتَىٰ يَبْدَأُ الدَّرْسُ؟

When does the lesson begin?

08أَيْنَ
where?
question word
أَيْنَ وَجَدْتَ هٰذَا الْكِتَابَ؟

Where did you find this book?

09كَيْفَ
how?
question word
كَيْفَ حَالُكَ؟

How are you? (lit. how is your state?)

10لِمَاذَا
why?
question wordلِـ + مَاذَا
لِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ؟

Why do you study Arabic?

Literally "for what?" — the preposition لِـ glued to مَاذَا. Arabic builds its "why" out of parts you already own.

11كَمْ
how many? how much?
question wordtakes a singular noun in naṣb
كَمْ كَلِمَةً حَفِظْتَ؟

How many words did you memorise?

Note the singular: كَمْ كَلِمَةً, not kam kalimātin. Arabic asks "how many word," and it puts that word in naṣb.

12هَلْ
(yes/no question marker)
particleno English equivalent
هَلْ فَهِمْتَ؟

Did you understand?

It has no meaning of its own — it simply announces that a question is coming, the way a rising tone does in English. Its cousin أَ does the same job in classical Arabic and in the Qur'an: أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ؟

13عَرَفَ
he knew, he recognised
verb · pastع – ر – فmaṣdar مَعْرِفَة
عَرَفْتُ الْجَوَابَ.

I knew the answer.

14قَدَرَ
he was able; he had power over
verb · pastق – د – رmaṣdar قُدْرَة
اللهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ.

Allah is capable of all things.

And there is فَعِيل again — the mould from Chapter 5, generating a divine name from a root you learned today.

15الْجَوَابُ
the answer, the reply
noun · masc.ج – و – بplural أَجْوِبَة
سُؤَالُكَ هُوَ الْجَوَابُ.

Your question is the answer.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
لَا أَفْهَمُ هٰذِهِ الْكَلِمَةَ.

I do not understand this word.

Present negation. Nothing changes on the verb.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the present — leaves the verb unharmed · حَرْف نَفْي— أَفْهَمُverb, present, I · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع هٰذِهِpointing word in object position · اِسْم إِشَارَةمَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا الْكَلِمَةَthe pointed-at noun · بَدَلمَنْصُوب
02
لَمْ أَفْهَمْ شَيْئًا.

I did not understand anything.

Past negation — but look at the verb: it is present in shape and clipped at the end. That is jazm.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَمْnegation of the past — cuts the verb · حَرْف نَفْي وَجَزْم— أَفْهَمْpresent in form, past in meaning — jazm · فِعْل مُضَارِع مَجْزُوممَجْزُوم شَيْئًاobject · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب
03
لَنْ أَنْسَىٰ هٰذَا الْيَوْمَ.

I will never forget this day.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَنْ"never" — naṣb on the verb · حَرْف نَفْي وَنَصْب— أَنْسَىٰdefective — the naṣb hides on the ā · فِعْل مُضَارِع مَنْصُوبمَنْصُوب هٰذَاpointing word in object position · اِسْم إِشَارَةمَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا الْيَوْمَthe pointed-at noun · بَدَلمَنْصُوب
04
مَنْ رَوَىٰ هٰذَا الْحَدِيثَ؟

Who narrated this hadith?

When you hear it: the standard question of a hadith class. The answer will be a name, then a chain.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَنْwho? — question word as subject · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَام · مُبْتَدَأمَبْنِيّ رَوَىٰverb, past, defective · فِعْل مَاضٍ— هٰذَاpointing word in object position · اِسْم إِشَارَةمَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا الْحَدِيثَthe pointed-at noun · بَدَلمَنْصُوب
05
مَاذَا يَعْنِي هٰذَا الْكَلَامُ؟

What does this statement mean?

Iʿrāb — word by word مَاذَاwhat? — question word in object position · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَاممَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا يَعْنِيverb, present, defective — the ḍamma hides on the ī · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع هٰذَاpointing word — the doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا الْكَلَامُthe pointed-at noun · بَدَلمَرْفُوع
06
لِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ؟

Why do you study Arabic?

You will be asked this. Have an answer ready: لِأَفْهَمَ كِتَابَ اللهِ — "so that I may understand the Book of Allah."

Iʿrāb — word by word لِمَاذَاwhy — li + mādhā · حَرْف جَرّ + اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَام— تَدْرُسُverb, present, you · فِعْل مُضَارِعمَرْفُوع الْعَرَبِيَّةَobject · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب
07
كَمْ كَلِمَةً حَفِظْتَ الْيَوْمَ؟

How many words did you memorise today?

Iʿrāb — word by word كَمْhow many · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَاممَبْنِيّ كَلِمَةًthe counted thing — singular, in naṣb · تَمْيِيزمَنْصُوب حَفِظْتَverb + you · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل— الْيَوْمَadverb of time · ظَرْف زَمَانمَنْصُوب
08
كَيْفَ حَالُكَ؟

How are you?

The universal greeting. Reply: بِخَيْرٍ، الْحَمْدُ لِلهِ — "well, praise be to Allah."

Iʿrāb — word by word كَيْفَhow? — the fronted news · اِسْم اِسْتِفْهَام · خَبَر مُقَدَّممَبْنِيّ حَالُكَsubject + your · مُبْتَدَأ + ضَمِيرمَرْفُوع
09
مَا قَالَ الشَّيْخُ شَيْئًا.

The shaykh did not say anything.

Past negation with مَا. Older, more classical, and very common in the Qur'an and hadith.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَاnegation of the past — no jazm needed · حَرْف نَفْي— قَالَverb, past, hollow · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الشَّيْخُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع شَيْئًاobject · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب
10
أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ؟

Did We not expand your breast for you?

Qur'anic. Look at it: أَ (question) + لَمْ (did not) + a jazm verb. Three things you learned today, in the opening of a sūrah.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَلَمْquestion + lam — "did not…?" · هَمْزَة الِاسْتِفْهَام + حَرْف جَزْم— نَشْرَحْWe — the sukūn of jazm · فِعْل مُضَارِع مَجْزُوممَجْزُوم لَكَpreposition + you · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور صَدْرَكَobject + your · مَفْعُول بِه + ضَمِيرمَنْصُوب

Section 4Grammar — Four Ways to Say No

English negates with one word and a helper: do not, did not, will not. Arabic has no helper verbs at all — so it uses a different particle for each stretch of time.

ParticleTimeVerb it takesExample
لَاpresentpresent · rafʿ (untouched)لَا يَفْهَمُ — he does not understand
مَاpastpast (untouched)مَا فَهِمَ — he did not understand
لَمْpastpresent · jazmلَمْ يَفْهَمْ — he did not understand
لَنْfuturepresent · naṣbلَنْ يَفْهَمَ — he will never understand
The one that breaks people

لَمْ يَفْهَمْ and مَا فَهِمَ mean exactly the same thing: "he did not understand." But one uses a past verb and the other uses a present verb with its ending cut off.

Beginners hear لَمْ يَفْهَمْ, recognise the present-tense shape, and translate "he does not understand." Wrong tense entirely. The tell is لَمْ itself — and the clipped, vowel-less ending that follows it. Train your ear on that little silence at the end of the verb. It is doing a lot of work.

And the three states of the verb from the masterclass, now in daylight:

يَفْهَمُ  ·  لَنْ يَفْهَمَ  ·  لَمْ يَفْهَمْ

rafʿ (-u) · naṣb (-a) · jazm (silence)

Asking a question changes nothing

Here is a small mercy. In English, a question turns the sentence inside out — you are becomes are you. In Arabic, you simply put a question word at the front and leave everything else exactly where it stands.

WordMeaningExample
هَلْ / أَyes-no markerهَلْ فَهِمْتَ؟
مَنْwhoمَنْ قَالَ هٰذَا؟
مَا / مَاذَاwhatمَاذَا تَقُولُ؟
مَتَىٰwhenمَتَىٰ رَجَعْتَ؟
أَيْنَwhereأَيْنَ الْكِتَابُ؟
كَيْفَhowكَيْفَ حَالُكَ؟
لِمَاذَاwhyلِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ؟
كَمْhow manyكَمْ كِتَابًا عِنْدَكَ؟
أَيّwhichأَيُّ كِتَابٍ؟
The rhetorical question is a lecturer's favourite tool

Half the questions you hear in a lecture are not questions. مَاذَا يَعْنِي هٰذَا؟ — "what does this mean?" — is almost never asking you. He is about to tell you.

Learn to hear that pattern, because it is a signal: a rhetorical question means the next sentence is the point. When you cannot follow everything, follow the questions — and listen hard to whatever comes immediately after one.

Section 5Reading Practice

لِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ؟

سَأَلَنِي رَجُلٌ: لِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ؟ أَنْتَ لَسْتَ عَرَبِيًّا.

فَقُلْتُ لَهُ: لِأَفْهَمَ كِتَابَ رَبِّي. هٰذَا هُوَ الْجَوَابُ.

قَالَ: وَلٰكِنَّ اللُّغَةَ صَعْبَةٌ. أَلَمْ تَجِدْ صُعُوبَةً؟

قُلْتُ: نَعَمْ، فِي الْأَوَّلِ لَمْ أَفْهَمْ شَيْئًا. مَا عَرَفْتُ كَلِمَةً وَاحِدَةً.

وَلٰكِنِّي مَا تَرَكْتُ الدَّرْسَ. صَبَرْتُ، وَالْآنَ أَفْهَمُ كَثِيرًا. وَلَنْ أَتْرُكَهَا أَبَدًا.

Why do you study Arabic?

A man asked me: why do you study Arabic? You are not an Arab.

So I said to him: so that I may understand the Book of my Lord. That is the answer.

He said: but the language is difficult. Did you not find difficulty?

I said: yes — at first I did not understand anything. I did not know a single word.

But I did not abandon the lesson. I was patient, and now I understand a great deal. And I will never abandon it.

New in context: لَسْتَ you are not · لِأَفْهَمَ so that I may understand · صُعُوبَة difficulty · تَرَكَ he left, abandoned · أَبَدًا ever, never

Four negations, one paragraph

لَسْتَ (you are not — from لَيْسَ) · لَمْ أَفْهَمْ (I did not understand — jazm) · مَا عَرَفْتُ (I did not know — past) · لَنْ أَتْرُكَ (I will never abandon — naṣb).

Four particles, four different times, four different effects on the verb. Read it again and watch each one land.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

مَنْ يَعْرِفُ مَعْنَىٰ «الْمَعْرُوفِ»؟ ... لَا أَحَدَ؟ طَيِّبٌ.

«الْمَعْرُوفُ» مِنْ «عَرَفَ». وَهُوَ الْخَيْرُ الَّذِي تَعْرِفُهُ الْقُلُوبُ.

وَلِمَاذَا سَمَّاهُ اللهُ «مَعْرُوفًا»؟ لِأَنَّ الْقَلْبَ السَّلِيمَ يَعْرِفُهُ. لَا يَحْتَاجُ إِلَىٰ دَلِيلٍ.

هَلْ فَهِمْتُمْ؟ هٰذِهِ فَائِدَةٌ عَظِيمَةٌ، وَلَنْ تَنْسَوْهَا.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَمَا «الْمُنْكَرُ»؟

الشَّيْخُ:

ضِدُّهُ. الَّذِي لَا تَعْرِفُهُ الْقُلُوبُ، وَتُنْكِرُهُ.

Shaykh: Who knows the meaning of al-maʿrūf? ... No one? Fine. Al-maʿrūf comes from عَرَفَ. It is the good that hearts recognise. And why did Allah call it maʿrūf — "the known"? Because the sound heart recognises it. It needs no proof. Have you understood? This is a tremendous benefit, and you will never forget it.

Student: Shaykh, and what is al-munkar?

Shaykh: Its opposite. That which hearts do not recognise — and reject.

New: لَا أَحَدَ no one · سَمَّىٰ he named · سَلِيم sound, whole · يَحْتَاجُ he needs · دَلِيل proof · ضِدّ opposite · يُنْكِرُ he rejects

Say it yourself

لِمَاذَا تَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ؟

لِأَفْهَمَ كِتَابَ رَبِّي.

لَمْ أَفْهَمْ، يَا شَيْخُ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb عَرَفَ
maṣdar مَعْرِفَة knowledge, cognition
to know, to recognise
Form I · sound · يَعْرِفُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIعَرَفْتُأَعْرِفُ——
نَحْنُweعَرَفْنَانَعْرِفُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)عَرَفْتَتَعْرِفُاِعْرِفْلَا تَعْرِفْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)عَرَفْتِتَعْرِفِينَاِعْرِفِيلَا تَعْرِفِي
هُوَheعَرَفَيَعْرِفُ——
هِيَsheعَرَفَتْتَعْرِفُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)عَرَفْتُمَاتَعْرِفَانِاِعْرِفَالَا تَعْرِفَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)عَرَفْتُمْتَعْرِفُونَاِعْرِفُوالَا تَعْرِفُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)عَرَفْتُمَاتَعْرِفَانِاِعْرِفَالَا تَعْرِفَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)عَرَفْتُنَّتَعْرِفْنَاِعْرِفْنَلَا تَعْرِفْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)عَرَفَايَعْرِفَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)عَرَفُوايَعْرِفُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)عَرَفَتَاتَعْرِفَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)عَرَفْنَيَعْرِفْنَ——

Distinguish it from عَلِمَ: عَرَفَ is to recognise, to be acquainted with — knowledge by encounter. عَلِمَ is to know — knowledge as fact. The Sufis built a whole vocabulary on the difference, calling their path مَعْرِفَة, not عِلْم.

verb قَدَرَ
maṣdar قُدْرَة power, ability
to be able, to have power over
Form I · sound · يَقْدِرُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIقَدَرْتُأَقْدِرُ——
نَحْنُweقَدَرْنَانَقْدِرُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)قَدَرْتَتَقْدِرُاِقْدِرْلَا تَقْدِرْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)قَدَرْتِتَقْدِرِينَاِقْدِرِيلَا تَقْدِرِي
هُوَheقَدَرَيَقْدِرُ——
هِيَsheقَدَرَتْتَقْدِرُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)قَدَرْتُمَاتَقْدِرَانِاِقْدِرَالَا تَقْدِرَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)قَدَرْتُمْتَقْدِرُونَاِقْدِرُوالَا تَقْدِرُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)قَدَرْتُمَاتَقْدِرَانِاِقْدِرَالَا تَقْدِرَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)قَدَرْتُنَّتَقْدِرْنَاِقْدِرْنَلَا تَقْدِرْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)قَدَرَايَقْدِرَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)قَدَرُوايَقْدِرُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)قَدَرَتَاتَقْدِرَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)قَدَرْنَيَقْدِرْنَ——

From this root: الْقَادِر and الْقَدِير (the All-Powerful), قَدَر (divine decree), and لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ. One root, and a whole theology of power and decree hangs off it.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to know / recogniseعَرَفَيَعْرِفُمَعْرِفَةعَارِفعُرِفَيُعْرَفُمَعْرُوفاِعْرِفْ
to be ableقَدَرَيَقْدِرُقُدْرَةقَادِرقُدِرَيُقْدَرُمَقْدُوراِقْدِرْ
One root through all eight moulds — عَرَفَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ع ر ف, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. عَرَفَ الْعَبْدُ رَبَّهُ. — The servant came to know his Lord.

المضارع · present active. يَعْرِفُ الْمُؤْمِنُ الْحَقَّ بِنُورِ الْإِيمَانِ. — The believer recognises truth by the light of faith.

المصدر · the action itself. مَعْرِفَةُ اللهِ أَشْرَفُ الْعُلُومِ. — Knowledge of Allah is the noblest of sciences.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْعَارِفُ بِاللهِ يَخْشَاهُ. — One who knows Allah fears Him.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. عُرِفَ الرَّجُلُ بِصِدْقِهِ. — The man was known for his truthfulness.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. يُعْرَفُ الْعَالِمُ بِعَمَلِهِ. — The scholar is known by his action.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. أَمَرَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَنَهَىٰ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ. — He enjoined the good (‘the known’) and forbade the evil.

الأمر · command. اِعْرِفِ اللهَ فِي الرَّخَاءِ. — Know Allah in times of ease.

The word the shaykh explained

مَعْرُوف — the done-to mould of عَرَفَ. Literally: that which is known. And it is the ordinary Arabic word for good, right conduct — as in الْأَمْرُ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ, "enjoining the good."

Sit with that. Arabic did not name the good after a commandment, or a benefit, or a law. It named it after recognition — the good is the thing hearts already know. An entire moral epistemology, folded into a grammatical mould, and you derived it yourself in a table.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

لَاnot (present) مَاnot (past); what لَمْdid not لَنْwill never مَنْwho? مَاذَاwhat? مَتَىٰwhen? أَيْنَwhere? كَيْفَhow? لِمَاذَاwhy? كَمْhow many? هَلْ(question marker) عَرَفَhe knew / recognised قَدَرَhe was able الْجَوَابُthe answer

Spiral recap — Chapters 1 & 5

عِنْدَat / with · to have الَّذِيwho / which كَبِيرbig, great صَعْبdifficult جَدِيدnew قَرِيبnear وَجَدَhe found ذَهَبَhe went
Before you close the book

You can now negate in every tense, and ask any question the language can ask. Which means: you can hold a conversation. Not a fluent one. But a real one — with questions, denials, and answers. That is not a small border to have crossed in a week.

End of Chapter 7Next → The One Who: Relative Clauses
Day 08 · Grammar: relative clauses & connectives

The One Who

Everything you have built so far makes sentences. This chapter makes paragraphs — the joints that let a speaker hang one idea off another and keep going for an hour without stopping. This is the grammar of argument.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Use the relative pronouns — الَّذِي · الَّتِي · الَّذِينَ — and learn the one rule about them that has no English equivalent at all.
  2. Learn the connective particles that carry an argument forward: لِأَنَّ · حَتَّىٰ · إِذَا · أَمَّا.
  3. Meet أَمَرَ — and complete the set of three hamza-dropping commands you were promised on Day 2.
  4. Learn شَرَحَ, whose maṣdar names half the classical library.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01الَّذِي
who, which, that (masc. sg.)
relative pronounfrozen · mabnī
هٰذَا هُوَ الرَّجُلُ الَّذِي قَالَ الْحَقَّ.

This is the man who spoke the truth.

02الَّتِي
who, which, that (fem. sg.)
relative pronoun
الْكَلِمَةُ الَّتِي حَفِظْتُهَا.

The word which I memorised.

Also used for non-human plurals, since those count as feminine singular. الْكُتُبُ الَّتِي قَرَأْتُهَا — "the books which I read."

03الَّذِينَ
those who (masc. pl.)
relative pronounTop 30 Qur'anic
الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ.

Those who believed and did righteous deeds.

Perhaps the single most frequent phrase-opener in the Qur'an. Learn it as a unit — you will meet it on nearly every page.

04لِأَنَّ
because
particlesister of إِنَّ → naṣb
أَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ لِأَنَّ الْقُرْآنَ عَرَبِيٌّ.

I study Arabic because the Qur'an is Arabic.

Look at the fatḥa on الْقُرْآنَ. لِأَنَّ is لِـ + أَنَّ — and أَنَّ is a sister of إِنَّ, so of course it takes naṣb. You predicted that.

05حَتَّىٰ
until; even; so that
particlethree jobs
صَبَرَ حَتَّىٰ أَصْبَحَ إِمَامًا.

He was patient until he became an imam.

06إِذَا
when, if (of something expected)
particle
إِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَتَدَبَّرْ.

When you read the Qur'an, reflect.

Notice the فَـ that opens the second half — Arabic often marks the "then" of a condition with it. إِذَا ... فَـ ... is a frame worth memorising whole.

07أَمَّا ... فَـ
as for ... , then ...
framethe lecturer's tool
أَمَّا الْعِلْمُ فَسَهْلٌ، وَأَمَّا الْعَمَلُ فَصَعْبٌ.

As for knowledge, it is easy; as for action, it is difficult.

Listening gold. When a speaker says أَمَّا, he is about to take one item from a list and deal with it. It means: a structured answer is coming.

08أَوْ
or
particle
اِقْرَأْ أَوِ اسْتَمِعْ.

Read, or listen.

09أَمَرَ
he commanded, he ordered
verb · pastأ – م – رcommand: مُرْ
أَمَرَ اللهُ بِالْعَدْلِ.

Allah commanded justice.

Note the shape: one commands with a thing — أَمَرَ بِـ. Hence الْأَمْرُ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ, "enjoining the good."

10شَرَحَ
he explained, he expounded
verb · pastش – ر – حmaṣdar شَرْح
شَرَحَ الشَّيْخُ الْحَدِيثَ.

The shaykh explained the hadith.

11الْمُؤْمِنُ
the believer
noun · doer of Form IVأ – م – نplural الْمُؤْمِنُونَ
الْمُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِ كَالْبُنْيَانِ.

The believer to the believer is like a building.

Note the مُـ prefix with a ḍamma — the signature of a derived-form doer. Same shape as مُدَرِّس (teacher) and مُسْلِم. Once you see it, you see it everywhere.

12الْحَدِيثُ
the hadith; speech, a report; new
noun · masc.ح – د – ثplural أَحَادِيث
هٰذَا حَدِيثٌ صَحِيحٌ.

This is an authentic hadith.

13السُّنَّةُ
the sunnah; the way, the practice
noun · fem.س – ن – ن
الْكِتَابُ وَالسُّنَّةُ.

The Book and the Sunnah.

14الْجَنَّةُ
the Garden, Paradise
noun · fem.ج – ن – ن
الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةُ.

Those who believed — for them is the Garden.

The root means to cover, to conceal — a garden is a place hidden by its own foliage. The same root gives جِنّ (the hidden ones) and مَجْنُون (one whose reason is veiled).

15الْيَوْمُ
the day; today
noun · masc.ي – و – مplural أَيَّام
يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ.

The Day of Standing (Resurrection).

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ.

Those who believed and did righteous deeds.

The Qur'an's most repeated phrase-opener. Learn it as one block of sound.

Iʿrāb — word by word الَّذِينَrelative noun — the subject here · اِسْم مَوْصُول مَبْنِيّمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا آمَنُواbelieved — verb + they; opens the ṣila · فِعْل مَاضٍ + وَاو الجَمَاعَة فَاعِل— وَعَمِلُواand did — joined onto the ṣila clause · مَعْطُوف · فِعْل + فَاعِل— الصَّالِحَاتِthe object — a sound feminine plural, so its naṣb shows as kasra · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب
02
أَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ لِأَنَّ الْقُرْآنَ عَرَبِيٌّ.

I study Arabic because the Qur'an is Arabic.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَدْرُسُI study — present; doer hidden ‘I’ · فِعْل مُضَارِع، فَاعِله مُسْتَتِرمَرْفُوع الْعَرَبِيَّةَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب لِأَنَّbecause — the lām of cause + a sister of inna · لَام التَّعْلِيل + أَنَّ— الْقُرْآنَthe noun of anna · اِسْم أَنَّمَنْصُوب عَرَبِيٌّthe news of anna · خَبَر أَنَّمَرْفُوع
03
أَمَّا الْعِلْمُ فَـسَهْلٌ، وَأَمَّا الْعَمَلُ فَـصَعْبٌ.

As for knowledge, it is easy; as for action, it is difficult.

The lecturer's structuring device. Hear أَمَّا and reach for your pen.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَمَّاas for — a detail-and-condition particle · حَرْف شَرْط وَتَفْصِيل— الْعِلْمُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع فَـthe fā that must answer أَمَّا · فَاء وَاقِعَة فِي جَوَاب أَمَّا— سَهْلٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع وَأَمَّاand as for — the second panel · حَرْف عَطْف + أَمَّا— الْعَمَلُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع فَـthe answering fā · فَاء الجَوَاب— صَعْبٌthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
04
هٰذَا هُوَ الرَّجُلُ الَّذِي شَرَحَ الْحَدِيثَ.

This is the man who explained the hadith.

Iʿrāb — word by word هٰذَاthis — a demonstrative, the topic · اِسْم إِشَارَة مَبْنِيّمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا هُوَa separating pronoun — no case of its own · ضَمِير فَصْل— الرَّجُلُthe news · خَبَرمَرْفُوع الَّذِيwho — relative noun describing the man · اِسْم مَوْصُول، نَعْتمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا شَرَحَexplained — verb, past; the ṣila has no address · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الْحَدِيثَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب
05
صَبَرَ حَتَّىٰ أَصْبَحَ إِمَامًا.

He was patient until he became an imam.

Iʿrāb — word by word صَبَرَwas patient — verb, past; doer hidden ‘he’ · فِعْل مَاضٍ— حَتَّىٰuntil — here a particle of limit, not hiding أَنْ · حَرْف اِبْتِدَاء وَغَايَة— أَصْبَحَbecame — a sister of kāna; its noun hidden ‘he’ · فِعْل مَاضٍ نَاقِص— إِمَامًاthe news of aṣbaḥa — that is why it is naṣb · خَبَر أَصْبَحَمَنْصُوب
06
إِذَا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ فَـتَدَبَّرْ.

When you read the Qur'an, reflect on it.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِذَاwhen — a condition particle for the future · ظَرْف شَرْط— قَرَأْتَyou read — past verb + you (doer) · فِعْل مَاضٍ + تَاء الفَاعِل— الْقُرْآنَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهمَنْصُوب فَـthe fā answering the condition · فَاء الجَوَاب— تَدَبَّرْreflect! — a command · فِعْل أَمْر مَبْنِيّ—
07
أَمَرَ اللهُ بِـالْعَدْلِ وَالْإِحْسَانِ.

Allah commanded justice and excellence.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَمَرَcommanded — verb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— اللهُthe doer · لَفْظ الجَلَالَة فَاعِلمَرْفُوع بِـwith — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْعَدْلِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور وَand — conjunction · حَرْف عَطْف— الْإِحْسَانِjoined onto al-ʿadl, so also jarr · مَعْطُوفمَجْرُور
08
الْكِتَابُ الَّذِي قَرَأْتُهُ قَدِيمٌ.

The book which I read is old.

Look at the ـهُ. Arabic says "the book which I read it." Section 4 explains why that pronoun is compulsory.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْكِتَابُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع الَّذِيwhich — relative noun, describing the book · اِسْم مَوْصُول، نَعْتمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا قَرَأْتُهُI read it — past verb + I + the returning ‘it’ · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل + هَاء عَائِد— قَدِيمٌthe news — after the clause closes · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
09
الْمُؤْمِنُ لِـلْمُؤْمِنِ كَـالْبُنْيَانِ.

The believer to the believer is like a building.

A hadith. Nominal sentence; the predicate is a prepositional phrase.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْمُؤْمِنُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع لِـto — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— لْمُؤْمِنِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور كَـlike — preposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْبُنْيَانِobject of ka-; the phrase is the news · اِسْم مَجْرُور، شِبْه الجُمْلَة خَبَرمَجْرُور
10
الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَهُمُ الْجَنَّةُ.

Those who believed — for them is the Garden.

Iʿrāb — word by word الَّذِينَthose who — relative noun, the topic · اِسْم مَوْصُول مَبْنِيّمَرْفُوع مَحَلًّا آمَنُواbelieved — the ṣila, no address · فِعْل مَاضٍ + فَاعِل— لَهُمُfor them — a prepositional phrase; the fronted news · جَارّ وَمَجْرُور، خَبَر مُقَدَّم— الْجَنَّةُthe delayed topic; the small sentence is خبر of الَّذِينَ · مُبْتَدَأ مُؤَخَّرمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — The One Who

Arabic's relative pronouns are, mercifully, frozen. They never change their endings — they are mabnī. But they must agree with the noun in gender and number.

FormUseExample
الَّذِيmasc. singularالرَّجُلُ الَّذِي قَالَ
الَّتِيfem. singular · and all non-human pluralsالْكُتُبُ الَّتِي قَرَأْتُهَا
الَّذِينَmasc. plural (people)الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا
The rule with no English equivalent

English says: the book which I read. Arabic says: the book which I read it.

الْكِتَابُ الَّذِي قَرَأْتُهُ

The clause must contain a pronoun pointing back at the noun. Grammarians call it the عَائِد — "the returner." It is not optional and it is not redundant: it is what stitches the clause to its noun. Leave it out and the sentence is broken.

English speakers forget it every single time for about a month. Then it becomes automatic, and you start hearing it in every relative clause a shaykh speaks.

And the one rule that saves you from a trap

The relative pronoun only appears when the noun is definite. If the noun is indefinite, Arabic drops it entirely:

الرَّجُلُ الَّذِي قَالَ ...

the man who said... (definite → pronoun present)

رَجُلٌ قَالَ ...

a man who said... (indefinite → no pronoun at all)

The clause is still a relative clause; Arabic simply does not bother to mark it. So when you hear an indefinite noun followed directly by a verb, do not assume a new sentence has started. It may well be describing the noun you just heard.

The joints of an argument

These are the words that turn sentences into reasoning. They are not decorative. They are the load-bearing structure of every lecture you will ever listen to.

WordMeaningWhat it signals
لِأَنَّbecausea reason follows
حَتَّىٰuntil · even · so thatan endpoint, or a limit
إِذَاwhen, ifa condition; expect فَـ to follow
أَمَّا ... فَـas for ... , then ...a structured list is being unpacked
فَـand so, thenimmediate consequence
ثُمَّthena gap, then the next thing
أَوْoran alternative
لٰكِنْbuta turn
The listening strategy that actually works

When you cannot follow a lecture word by word — and for months you will not be able to — follow the joints.

لِأَنَّ tells you a reason is coming. أَمَّا tells you a list is being unpacked. حَتَّىٰ tells you the story has reached its point. فَـ tells you a consequence follows.

You can lose most of the nouns and still know the shape of what is being argued. That skeleton is what carries you until the vocabulary arrives — and the vocabulary always arrives.

Section 5Reading Practice

الْعِلْمُ وَالْعَمَلُ

الْعَالِمُ هُوَ الَّذِي يَعْمَلُ بِعِلْمِهِ. أَمَّا الَّذِي يَعْرِفُ وَلَا يَعْمَلُ، فَلَيْسَ بِعَالِمٍ.

قَالَ رَجُلٌ لِلشَّيْخِ: قَرَأْتُ الْكُتُبَ الَّتِي أَمَرْتَنِي بِهَا، وَحَفِظْتُ الْأَحَادِيثَ الَّتِي شَرَحْتَهَا.

فَقَالَ الشَّيْخُ: وَهَلْ عَمِلْتَ؟

فَسَكَتَ الرَّجُلُ.

قَالَ الشَّيْخُ: يَا بُنَيَّ، الْعِلْمُ الَّذِي لَا عَمَلَ مَعَهُ كَشَجَرَةٍ بِلَا ثَمَرٍ. اِعْمَلْ، حَتَّىٰ يَصِيرَ عِلْمُكَ نُورًا.

Knowledge and Action

The scholar is the one who acts by his knowledge. As for the one who knows and does not act — he is no scholar.

A man said to the shaykh: I read the books which you commanded me [to read], and I memorised the hadiths which you explained.

So the shaykh said: and did you act?

The man fell silent.

The shaykh said: my son, knowledge with no action alongside it is like a tree without fruit. Act — until your knowledge becomes a light.

New in context: سَكَتَ he fell silent · مَعَهُ with it · شَجَرَة tree · ثَمَر fruit

Find the returners

الْكُتُبَ الَّتِي أَمَرْتَنِي بِهَا — "the books which you commanded me with them."

الْأَحَادِيثَ الَّتِي شَرَحْتَهَا — "the hadiths which you explained them."

Both plurals are non-human, so both take الَّتِي and both return with ـهَا. Two rules from two different chapters, cooperating in a single phrase. This is what it feels like when the machinery starts running by itself.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

الْيَوْمَ نَشْرَحُ حَدِيثًا قَصِيرًا، وَلٰكِنَّهُ عَظِيمٌ.

قَالَ الرَّسُولُ ﷺ: «الْمُؤْمِنُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِ كَالْبُنْيَانِ، يَشُدُّ بَعْضُهُ بَعْضًا».

أَمَّا مَعْنَاهُ فَوَاضِحٌ. وَأَمَّا الْفَائِدَةُ فَهِيَ الَّتِي نَطْلُبُهَا.

لِأَنَّ الْبُنْيَانَ لَا يَقُومُ بِحَجَرٍ وَاحِدٍ. وَالْأُمَّةُ لَا تَقُومُ بِرَجُلٍ وَاحِدٍ.

فَإِذَا ضَعُفَ أَخُوكَ، فَاشْدُدْهُ. هٰذَا هُوَ الْحَدِيثُ، وَهٰذَا هُوَ الْعَمَلُ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، مَنْ رَوَىٰ هٰذَا الْحَدِيثَ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

رَوَاهُ الْبُخَارِيُّ وَمُسْلِمٌ. وَهُوَ صَحِيحٌ.

Shaykh: Today we explain a short hadith — but a tremendous one. The Messenger ﷺ said: "The believer to the believer is like a building; each part strengthens the other." As for its meaning, that is clear. As for the benefit — that is what we are seeking. Because a building does not stand on a single stone. And a community does not stand on a single man. So if your brother weakens, strengthen him. This is the hadith, and this is the action.

Student: Shaykh, who narrated this hadith?

Shaykh: Al-Bukhārī and Muslim narrated it. And it is authentic.

New: الْبُنْيَان a building · يَشُدُّ it strengthens · بَعْضُهُ بَعْضًا one part the other · يَقُومُ it stands · حَجَر stone · ضَعُفَ he became weak · أَخُوكَ your brother

Listen for the joints

Read that lecture again and mark only the connectives: وَلٰكِنَّهُ · أَمَّا ... فَـ · وَأَمَّا ... فَـ · لِأَنَّ · فَإِذَا ... فَـ.

Strip out every noun and you can still see the argument: here is a thing — but note this — as for A, X; as for B, Y — because of a reason — so, if C, then do D.

That is the shape of an argument, and you just read it in Arabic without needing a single one of the nouns. This is the skill that will carry you into real lectures long before your vocabulary is ready.

Say it yourself

هٰذَا هُوَ الْكِتَابُ الَّذِي قَرَأْتُهُ.

أَدْرُسُ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ لِأَنَّ الْقُرْآنَ عَرَبِيٌّ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb أَمَرَ

The third of the three hamza-dropping commands you were promised in Chapter 2: خُذْ (take), كُلْ (eat), and now مُرْ (command). You have now met all three.
