قَالَ الرَّسُولُ ﷺ: إِذَا جَاءَ رَمَضَانُ، فُتِحَتْ أَبْوَابُ الْجَنَّةِ، وَغُلِّقَتْ أَبْوَابُ النَّارِ، وَصُفِّدَتِ الشَّيَاطِينُ.

اُنْظُرْ إِلَى الْأَفْعَالِ: فُتِحَتْ، غُلِّقَتْ، صُفِّدَتْ. مَنِ الَّذِي فَتَحَ؟ وَمَنِ الَّذِي غَلَّقَ؟

لَمْ يَقُلْ. وَلَا يَحْتَاجُ أَنْ يَقُولَ.

وَهٰذَا هُوَ الْمَجْهُولُ: لَيْسَ الْفَاعِلُ مَجْهُولًا عِنْدَنَا، وَلٰكِنَّهُ مَحْذُوفٌ مِنَ الْكَلَامِ.

فَإِذَا سَمِعْتَ فِعْلًا مَبْنِيًّا لِلْمَجْهُولِ، فَاسْأَلْ: لِمَاذَا حُذِفَ الْفَاعِلُ؟ وَفِي الْجَوَابِ عِلْمٌ كَثِيرٌ.

When Ramaḍān Comes

The Messenger ﷺ said: when Ramaḍān comes, the gates of Paradise are opened, and the gates of the Fire are closed, and the devils are chained.

Look at the verbs: were opened, were closed, were chained. Who was it that opened? And who was it that closed?

He did not say. And he does not need to say.

And this is al-majhūl: the doer is not unknown to us — but he has been deleted from the speech.

So when you hear a verb built for the unknown, ask: why was the doer deleted? And in the answer there is a great deal of knowledge.

New in context: غُلِّقَتْ were closed · صُفِّدَتْ were chained · مَحْذُوف deleted · حُذِفَ it was deleted

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

يَقُولُ اللهُ تَعَالَىٰ: «كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ».

وَلَمْ يَقُلْ: «كَتَبْتُ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامَ». فَلِمَاذَا؟

قَالَ الْعُلَمَاءُ: لِأَنَّ الْحُكْمَ ثَابِتٌ، وَلَوْ لَمْ يُذْكَرِ الْفَاعِلُ. الصِّيَامُ مَكْتُوبٌ، وَانْتَهَى الْأَمْرُ.

وَقَالَ بَعْضُهُمْ: بَلْ فِيهِ رَحْمَةٌ. لِأَنَّ الْأَمْرَ إِذَا جَاءَ بِلَا اسْمِ الْآمِرِ، كَانَ أَخَفَّ عَلَى النَّفْسِ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، فَهَلْ لِكُلِّ مَجْهُولٍ سَبَبٌ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

فِي كَلَامِ اللهِ، نَعَمْ. وَفِي كَلَامِ النَّاسِ، أَحْيَانًا كَسَلٌ.

Shaykh: Allah says: "Fasting has been prescribed upon you." And He did not say: "I have prescribed fasting upon you." So why? The scholars said: because the ruling is established, even if the doer is not mentioned. Fasting is written — and the matter is finished. And some of them said: rather, there is a mercy in it. Because a command that arrives without the name of the commander sits more lightly on the soul.

Student: Shaykh — so does every passive have a reason?

Shaykh: In the speech of Allah, yes. In the speech of people — sometimes it is just laziness.

New: الصِّيَام fasting · ثَابِت established · لَمْ يُذْكَرْ is not mentioned · أَخَفّ lighter · كَسَل laziness

Say it yourself

Three passives — two of them straight from the Qur'an. Listen for the u–i signature as you say them.

خُلِقَ الْإِنْسَانُ ضَعِيفًا.

كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ.

قِيلَ لِي هٰذَا مِنْ قَبْلُ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb فَتَحَ
maṣdar فَتْح opening, conquest
to open; to conquer
Form I · sound · يَفْتَحُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIفَتَحْتُأَفْتَحُ——
نَحْنُweفَتَحْنَانَفْتَحُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)فَتَحْتَتَفْتَحُاِفْتَحْلَا تَفْتَحْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)فَتَحْتِتَفْتَحِينَاِفْتَحِيلَا تَفْتَحِي
هُوَheفَتَحَيَفْتَحُ——
هِيَsheفَتَحَتْتَفْتَحُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)فَتَحْتُمَاتَفْتَحَانِاِفْتَحَالَا تَفْتَحَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)فَتَحْتُمْتَفْتَحُونَاِفْتَحُوالَا تَفْتَحُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)فَتَحْتُمَاتَفْتَحَانِاِفْتَحَالَا تَفْتَحَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)فَتَحْتُنَّتَفْتَحْنَاِفْتَحْنَلَا تَفْتَحْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)فَتَحَايَفْتَحَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)فَتَحُوايَفْتَحُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)فَتَحَتَاتَفْتَحَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)فَتَحْنَيَفْتَحْنَ——

الْفَاتِحَة — “the Opener” — is the doer-mould of this verb, feminine. The first sūrah is not called the beginning; it is called the one that opens. And فَتْح مَكَّة is the opening of Mecca, not its capture. The word chooses its own theology.

verb رَزَقَ
maṣdar رِزْق provision, sustenance
to provide, to grant sustenance
Form I · sound · يَرْزُقُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIرَزَقْتُأَرْزُقُ——
نَحْنُweرَزَقْنَانَرْزُقُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)رَزَقْتَتَرْزُقُاُرْزُقْلَا تَرْزُقْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)رَزَقْتِتَرْزُقِينَاُرْزُقِيلَا تَرْزُقِي
هُوَheرَزَقَيَرْزُقُ——
هِيَsheرَزَقَتْتَرْزُقُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)رَزَقْتُمَاتَرْزُقَانِاُرْزُقَالَا تَرْزُقَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)رَزَقْتُمْتَرْزُقُونَاُرْزُقُوالَا تَرْزُقُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)رَزَقْتُمَاتَرْزُقَانِاُرْزُقَالَا تَرْزُقَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)رَزَقْتُنَّتَرْزُقْنَاُرْزُقْنَلَا تَرْزُقْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)رَزَقَايَرْزُقَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)رَزَقُوايَرْزُقُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)رَزَقَتَاتَرْزُقَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)رَزَقْنَيَرْزُقْنَ——

الرَّزَّاق, the Provider, is this root in the intensive mould فَعَّال — the same mould as غَفَّار and قَهَّار. And its passive, رُزِقَ, is how Arabic says someone was granted a child, a livelihood, a gift — with the giver left unnamed, because everyone knows.

verb بَنَىٰ
maṣdar بِنَاء building, construction
to build
Form I · defective · يَبْنِي
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIبَنَيْتُأَبْنِي——
نَحْنُweبَنَيْنَانَبْنِي——
أَنْتَyou (m.)بَنَيْتَتَبْنِياِبْنِلَا تَبْنِ
أَنْتِyou (f.)بَنَيْتِتَبْنِينَاِبْنِيلَا تَبْنِي
هُوَheبَنَىٰيَبْنِي——
هِيَsheبَنَتْتَبْنِي——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)بَنَيْتُمَاتَبْنِيَانِاِبْنِيَالَا تَبْنِيَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)بَنَيْتُمْتَبْنُونَاِبْنُوالَا تَبْنُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)بَنَيْتُمَاتَبْنِيَانِاِبْنِيَالَا تَبْنِيَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)بَنَيْتُنَّتَبْنِينَاِبْنِينَلَا تَبْنِينَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)بَنَيَايَبْنِيَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)بَنَوْايَبْنُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)بَنَتَاتَبْنِيَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)بَنَيْنَيَبْنِينَ——

Its passive gives you the sentence you met on Day 12: بُنِيَ الْإِسْلَامُ عَلَىٰ خَمْسٍ — “Islam was built upon five.” Watch how the weak final letter behaves in the passive: banā → buniya.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to open / conquerفَتَحَيَفْتَحُفَتْحفَاتِحفُتِحَيُفْتَحُمَفْتُوحاِفْتَحْ
to provideرَزَقَيَرْزُقُرِزْقرَازِقرُزِقَيُرْزَقُمَرْزُوقاُرْزُقْ
to buildبَنَىٰيَبْنِيبِنَاءبَانٍبُنِيَيُبْنَىٰمَبْنِيّاِبْنِ
One root through all eight moulds — فَتَحَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ف ت ح, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. فَتَحَ اللهُ عَلَىٰ نَبِيِّهِ. — Allah granted victory to His Prophet.

المضارع · present active. يَفْتَحُ اللهُ أَبْوَابَ الرَّحْمَةِ. — Allah opens the gates of mercy.

المصدر · the action itself. إِذَا جَاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ وَالْفَتْحُ. — When the help of Allah and the conquest come.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْفَاتِحُ لِلْقُلُوبِ هُوَ اللهُ. — The One who opens hearts is Allah.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. فُتِحَتْ مَكَّةُ فِي رَمَضَانَ. — Mecca was conquered in Ramaḍān.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. تُفْتَحُ أَبْوَابُ الْجَنَّةِ فِي رَمَضَانَ. — The gates of Paradise are opened in Ramaḍān.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْبَابُ مَفْتُوحٌ لِلتَّائِبِينَ. — The door is open for those who repent.

الأمر · command. اِفْتَحْ قَلْبَكَ لِلْقُرْآنِ. — Open your heart to the Qur’an.

Read the passive columns, and only those

فُتِحَ · رُزِقَ · بُنِيَ — and hear the identical music: u–i. Three different roots, three different verb types (sound, sound, defective), and the same two vowels doing the same job.

That is the sound of a hidden agent. Learn it, and you will never again mistake it was written for he wrote.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

فَتَحَhe openedرَزَقَhe provided بَنَىٰhe builtالْمَجْهُولُthe passive نَائِبُ الْفَاعِلِpassive subjectالْبَابُthe door, chapter الرِّزْقُprovisionالْأَجْرُthe reward الْقَبْرُthe graveالشَّيْطَانُthe devil مَكْتُوبwritten, decreedمَعْرُوفthe known / good دُفِنَhe was buriedقِيلَit was said يُقَالُit is said

Spiral recap — Chapters 9, 11 & 14

عَلَّمَhe taught (II)أَنْزَلَhe sent down (IV) أَسْلَمَhe submitted (IV)الظُّلْمَةُdarkness قَلْب — قُلُوبheart(s)مَسْجِد — مَسَاجِدmosque(s) كَتَمَhe concealedرَفَعَhe raised
Before you close the book

Arabic did not call this "the passive." It called it the unknown — naming the construction after the person who is missing from it.

That is a habit of mind worth borrowing. From now on, when you hear a verb in u–i, do not merely translate it. Ask the question the shaykh asked: why was the doer deleted? Ninety per cent of the time, the answer is the point of the sentence.

End of Chapter 17Next → Ḥāl and Tamyīz: The Accusative of Circumstance
Day 18 · Grammar: ḥāl and tamyīz

The Accusative
of Everything Else

You have known since the masterclass that naṣb is the bin for "everything extra." Today we open that bin. Inside are the two constructions that carry most of the descriptive weight of Arabic prose — and both are invisible to a reader who does not know they exist.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Master the ḥāl (الحَال) — the accusative that says in what state something happened.
  2. Master the tamyīz (التَّمْيِيز) — the accusative that says in what respect a statement is true.
  3. Tell them apart instantly, using one question.
  4. Understand why so many Arabic fatḥas have no equivalent word in English at all.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

Why these two matter so much

English carries this information with prepositions and subordinate clauses: while he was laughing, in terms of knowledge, as regards his soul. Arabic carries it with a single fatḥa on a single word.

Which means: a reader who does not know the ḥāl will see ضَاحِكًا and think it is a stray object. He will not merely miss a nuance — he will mis-parse the sentence. These two constructions are not ornament. They are load-bearing.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01جَاءَ
he came
verb · hollowج – ي – أTop 30
جَاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْبَاطِلُ.

The truth has come and falsehood has vanished.

The natural home of the ḥāl: one comes in some state — running, weeping, laughing, alone.

02ضَحِكَ
he laughed
verb · soundض – ح – كḥāl: ضَاحِكًا
جَاءَ ضَاحِكًا.

He came laughing.

03بَكَىٰ
he wept
verb · defectiveب – ك – يḥāl: بَاكِيًا
خَرَجَ مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ بَاكِيًا.

He left the mosque weeping.

04الحَالُ
the state, the condition — the circumstantial accusative
grammar termalways naṣb
كَيْفَ حَالُكَ؟

How are you? (literally: how is your state?)

You already knew this word as "condition" — from the greeting you have used for years. Now it becomes a grammar term, and the two senses are the same sense.

05التَّمْيِيزُ
the specification — the accusative of respect
grammar termalways naṣbم – ي – ز
اِزْدَدْتُ عِلْمًا.

I increased in knowledge.

The root means to distinguish, to sort out. A tamyīz distinguishes which of several possible meanings a vague statement carries.

06قَائِم
standing; upright; established
doer-nounق – و – م
قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ قَائِمًا.

He recited the Qur'an standing.

07جَالِس
sitting
doer-nounج – ل – س
صَلَّىٰ جَالِسًا.

He prayed sitting.

Note the shape. Every ḥāl in this chapter is a فَاعِل-mould noun — the doer-mould from your derivation tables — simply put into naṣb. You are not learning new words. You are reusing a mould in a new slot.

08صَامِت
silent
doer-nounص – م – ت
خَرَجُوا صَامِتِينَ.

They left in silence.

09وَحْدَهُ
alone, by himself
ḥāl · frozen in naṣb
لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَحْدَهُ.

There is no god but Allah, alone.

Permanently in naṣb, wherever it appears — because it is permanently a ḥāl. You met it on Day 3 in الْعِلْمُ لَا يَأْتِي وَحْدَهُ without knowing why it had a fatḥa. Now you do.

10جَمِيعًا
all together
ḥāl · frozen in naṣb
اِعْتَصِمُوا بِحَبْلِ اللهِ جَمِيعًا.

Hold fast to the rope of Allah, all together.

11الْبَاطِلُ
falsehood, the void
nounب – ط – ل
جَاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْبَاطِلُ.

The truth has come and falsehood has vanished.

The permanent opposite of الْحَقّ, which you learned on Day 1. Learn opposites in pairs; the memory files them together.

12شَهِيد
a witness; a martyr
noun · mould فَعِيلش – ه – د
كَفَىٰ بِاللهِ شَهِيدًا.

Allah suffices as a witness.

Both meanings at once, and the connection is exact: a martyr is one who bore witness with his life. Same فَعِيل mould from Day 5.

13مُبَكِّر
early
doer of IIب – ك – ر
جِئْتُ مُبَكِّرًا.

I came early.

14اِزْدَادَ
he increased, he grew
verb · Form VIIIز – ي – د
رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْمًا.

My Lord, increase me in knowledge.

The Qur'anic duʿā of the seeker of knowledge — and its final word, عِلْمًا, is a tamyīz. Increase me — in respect of what? — knowledge.

15كَفَىٰ
it sufficed, it was enough
verb · defectiveك – ف – ي
كَفَىٰ بِاللهِ شَهِيدًا.

Allah suffices as a witness.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

From this chapter on, every sentence is broken down word by word. Read the Arabic first, guess the roles from the colours, then check yourself against the table underneath.

01
جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ ضَاحِكًا.

The student came laughing.

The chapter in one sentence. ضَاحِكًا is not an adjective describing the student in general — it describes the state he was in at the moment of coming. And it wears a fatḥa.

Iʿrāb — word by word جَاءَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الطَّالِبُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع ضَاحِكًاthe circumstance — how he came · حَالمَنْصُوب
02
رَجَعَ مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ بَاكِيًا.

He returned from the mosque weeping.

Iʿrāb — word by word رَجَعَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— مِنَpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْمَسْجِدِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور بَاكِيًاthe circumstance · حَالمَنْصُوب
03
قَرَأَ الْقُرْآنَ قَائِمًا.

He recited the Qur'an standing.

Two fatḥas, two different jobs. الْقُرْآنَ is what he recited; قَائِمًا is how he was while reciting. Both in naṣb — which is exactly why naṣb is the case of the “extras.”

Iʿrāb — word by word قَرَأَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الْقُرْآنَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب قَائِمًاthe circumstance · حَالمَنْصُوب
04
اِزْدَدْتُ عِلْمًا.

I increased — in knowledge.

This is tamyīz. The verb increased is vague until the tamyīz arrives and says: in what respect. English needs a preposition; Arabic just uses a fatḥa.

Iʿrāb — word by word اِزْدَدْتُverb, past, 1st person · فِعْل مَاضٍ— عِلْمًاthe specifier — in respect of what? · تَمْيِيزمَنْصُوب
05
اللهُ أَكْبَرُ.

Allah is greater.

A nominal sentence. No verb. Both halves in rafʿ — which is why they are both teal.

Iʿrāb — word by word اللهُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع أَكْبَرُwhat is said about it · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
06
جِئْتُ مُبَكِّرًا.

I came early.

Iʿrāb — word by word جِئْتُverb, past, 1st person · فِعْل مَاضٍ— مُبَكِّرًاthe circumstance · حَالمَنْصُوب
07
طِبْتَ نَفْسًا.

You are content — in soul.

A classical idiom, and pure tamyīz. Arabic says “you are good as to soul”; English says “you are content.”

Iʿrāb — word by word طِبْتَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— نَفْسًاthe specifier · تَمْيِيزمَنْصُوب
08
خَرَجَ النَّاسُ مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ صَامِتِينَ.

The people left the mosque in silence.

The ḥāl agrees with its owner. The people are plural, so the ḥāl is plural too — and a sound masculine plural in naṣb ends in ـِينَ. Chapter 11, still holding.

Iʿrāb — word by word خَرَجَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— النَّاسُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع مِنَpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— الْمَسْجِدِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور صَامِتِينَthe circumstance (plural) · حَالمَنْصُوب
09
كَفَىٰ بِاللهِ شَهِيدًا.

Allah suffices as a witness.

A famous construction. The بِـ is grammatically redundant — it adds no meaning, only weight. The word after it is really the doer, but wears a kasra because the preposition insists. Classical Arabic does this for emphasis, and it does it often.

Iʿrāb — word by word كَفَىٰverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— بِاللهِthe doer, dragged in by a redundant بِـ · فَاعِل · مَجْرُور لَفْظًامَجْرُور شَهِيدًاthe specifier · تَمْيِيزمَنْصُوب
10
لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

There is no god but Allah.

Tomorrow's chapter, arriving early. Look at the two endings: إِلٰهَ in naṣb, اللهُ in rafʿ. That single difference of vowel is the entire grammar of the shahādah — and Chapter 19 will show you why it could not have been otherwise.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة لِلْجِنْس— إِلٰهَits noun · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب إِلَّاthe exception particle · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— اللهُthe excepted one · بَدَل · مَرْفُوعمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — Two Kinds of Extra

Part one: the ḥāl — in what state?

A ḥāl is a word in naṣb that describes the condition of someone at the moment the verb happened. English usually needs a whole clause for this — while he was laughing — and Arabic needs one word.

جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ ضَاحِكًا.

The student came laughing.

Three rules govern it, and all three are worth stating precisely:

RuleDetail
1. The ḥāl is always naṣbAlways. ضَاحِكًا, بَاكِيًا, قَائِمًا — every one wears a fatḥa.
2. The ḥāl is indefiniteNever الـ. This is what distinguishes it from an adjective.
3. Its owner (صَاحِب الحَال) is usually definiteالطَّالِبُ — definite. The ḥāl — indefinite. The mismatch is the signal.
The distinction that decides your parse

جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ الضَّاحِكُ — "the laughing student came." Both definite, both rafʿ → this is a noun + adjective. The laughing is a permanent property of the student.

جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ ضَاحِكًا — "the student came laughing." Definite, then indefinite in naṣb → this is a ḥāl. The laughing is a temporary state, true only at the moment of arrival.

Same two words. One vowel apart. And the difference is between describing a person and describing a moment.

Part two: the tamyīz — in what respect?

A tamyīz is a word in naṣb that resolves an ambiguity. The sentence before it is vague; the tamyīz arrives and specifies the respect in which it is true.

اِزْدَدْتُ عِلْمًا.

I increased — in knowledge.

"I increased" — in what? Wealth? Height? Age? The verb alone does not say. Then عِلْمًا lands, and the ambiguity is gone. That is the tamyīz's entire job: it is a word whose function is to close a question the sentence has just opened.

You have already met it three times without knowing:

SentenceThe tamyīzThe question it answers
عِشْرُونَ كِتَابًاكِتَابًاTwenty what?
كَفَىٰ بِاللهِ شَهِيدًاشَهِيدًاSuffices as what?
رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْمًاعِلْمًاIncrease me in what?
One question tells them apart

Both are indefinite nouns in naṣb. So how do you know which you are looking at? Ask:

"In what state?" → it is a ḥāl. He came — in what state? — laughing.

"In what respect?" → it is a tamyīz. I increased — in what respect? — knowledge.

A ḥāl describes a person. A tamyīz clarifies a statement. That is the whole of it.

Why Arabic does it this way

It is worth pausing on how economical this is. English has to build a whole subordinate clause — while he was laughing — with a conjunction, a pronoun and a verb, three words of scaffolding to carry one piece of information.

Arabic already has a spare case sitting there. Naṣb is not doing anything else. So Arabic simply puts the information in the case, and the scaffolding disappears. One word, one vowel, and the job is done.

This is why classical Arabic prose is so dense, and why a translation is always longer than its original. The endings are carrying meaning that English has to spell out in words.

Section 5Reading Practice

رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْمًا

دَخَلَ الطَّالِبُ الْمَسْجِدَ مُبَكِّرًا، وَجَلَسَ صَامِتًا، وَاسْتَمَعَ إِلَى الشَّيْخِ.

قَرَأَ الشَّيْخُ الْآيَةَ قَائِمًا، ثُمَّ جَلَسَ وَشَرَحَهَا.

قَالَ: مَا أَمَرَ اللهُ نَبِيَّهُ ﷺ أَنْ يَطْلُبَ الزِّيَادَةَ فِي شَيْءٍ إِلَّا فِي الْعِلْمِ.

«وَقُلْ رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْمًا». اُنْظُرُوا: «عِلْمًا» تَمْيِيزٌ. أَيْ: زِدْنِي — فِي أَيِّ شَيْءٍ؟ — فِي الْعِلْمِ.

فَخَرَجَ الطَّالِبُ مِنَ الدَّرْسِ بَاكِيًا، وَقَدِ ازْدَادَ عِلْمًا وَخَشْيَةً.

My Lord, Increase Me in Knowledge

The student entered the mosque early, sat in silence, and listened to the shaykh.

The shaykh recited the verse standing, then sat and explained it.

He said: Allah did not command His Prophet ﷺ to seek increase in anything except in knowledge.

"And say: my Lord, increase me in knowledge." Look: ʿilman is a tamyīz. That is: increase me — in what thing? — in knowledge.

So the student left the lesson weeping, having increased in knowledge and in awe.

New in context: الزِّيَادَة the increase · أَيْ that is to say · خَشْيَة awe, reverent fear

Six ḥāls in five lines

مُبَكِّرًا (early) · صَامِتًا (silent) · قَائِمًا (standing) · بَاكِيًا (weeping) — and two tamyīzes: عِلْمًا and خَشْيَةً.

Six fatḥas that are not objects. Two weeks ago you would have read every one of them as a direct object and mis-parsed every sentence they appeared in.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

يَسْأَلُنِي الطُّلَّابُ: لِمَاذَا هٰذِهِ الْفَتْحَةُ؟ الْكَلِمَةُ لَيْسَتْ مَفْعُولًا بِهِ!

فَأَقُولُ: صَدَقْتَ. وَلٰكِنَّ النَّصْبَ لَيْسَ لِلْمَفْعُولِ وَحْدَهُ.

النَّصْبُ بَيْتُ الزِّيَادَاتِ. كَيْفَ؟ وَمَتَىٰ؟ وَلِمَاذَا؟ وَكَمْ؟ وَفِي أَيِّ شَيْءٍ؟

كُلُّهَا تَسْكُنُ فِي النَّصْبِ. فَإِذَا سَمِعْتَ فَتْحَةً وَلَمْ تَجِدْ لَهَا مَفْعُولًا، فَاسْأَلْ: أَهِيَ حَالٌ؟ أَمْ تَمْيِيزٌ؟ أَمْ ظَرْفٌ؟

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، وَكَيْفَ أُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ الْحَالِ وَالتَّمْيِيزِ؟

الشَّيْخُ:

الْحَالُ تَصِفُ صَاحِبَهَا. وَالتَّمْيِيزُ يُفَسِّرُ الْجُمْلَةَ. وَاحِدَةٌ تَصِفُ رَجُلًا، وَالْأُخْرَىٰ تَصِفُ مَعْنًى.

Shaykh: The students ask me: why this fatḥa? The word is not a direct object! And I say: you are right. But naṣb does not belong to the object alone. Naṣb is the house of the extras. How? When? Why? How many? In what respect? All of them dwell in naṣb. So when you hear a fatḥa and cannot find an object for it, ask: is it a ḥāl? A tamyīz? An adverb of time?

Student: Shaykh, and how do I distinguish the ḥāl from the tamyīz?

Shaykh: The ḥāl describes its owner. The tamyīz explains the sentence. One describes a man; the other describes a meaning.

New: فَتْحَة a fatḥa · الزِّيَادَات the extras · تَسْكُنُ they dwell · أُفَرِّقُ I distinguish · تَصِفُ she describes · يُفَسِّرُ it explains

Say it yourself

Three accusatives in the wild. Say them aloud and name the naṣb as you go.

فَرِحْتُ فَرَحًا شَدِيدًا.

رَجَعَ الشَّيْخُ مُبْتَسِمًا.

خَرَجْتُ مِنَ الدَّرْسِ سَعِيدًا.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb جَاءَ
maṣdar مَجِيء coming
to come
Form I · hollow (ي) · يَجِيءُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIجِئْتُأَجِيءُ——
نَحْنُweجِئْنَانَجِيءُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)جِئْتَتَجِيءُجِئْلَا تَجِئْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)جِئْتِتَجِيئِينَجِيئِيلَا تَجِيئِي
هُوَheجَاءَيَجِيءُ——
هِيَsheجَاءَتْتَجِيءُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)جِئْتُمَاتَجِيئَانِجِيئَالَا تَجِيئَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)جِئْتُمْتَجِيئُونَجِيئُوالَا تَجِيئُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)جِئْتُمَاتَجِيئَانِجِيئَالَا تَجِيئَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)جِئْتُنَّتَجِئْنَجِئْنَلَا تَجِئْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)جَاءَايَجِيئَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)جَاءُوايَجِيئُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)جَاءَتَاتَجِيئَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)جِئْنَيَجِئْنَ——

Hollow, and hamzated on top — so it bends twice. But the machinery is the one you know from قَالَ: the long vowel survives before a vowel-ending and shortens before a consonant. jāʾa → jiʾtu.

verb ضَحِكَ
maṣdar ضَحِك laughter
to laugh
Form I · sound · يَضْحَكُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIضَحِكْتُأَضْحَكُ——
نَحْنُweضَحِكْنَانَضْحَكُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)ضَحِكْتَتَضْحَكُاِضْحَكْلَا تَضْحَكْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)ضَحِكْتِتَضْحَكِينَاِضْحَكِيلَا تَضْحَكِي
هُوَheضَحِكَيَضْحَكُ——
هِيَsheضَحِكَتْتَضْحَكُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)ضَحِكْتُمَاتَضْحَكَانِاِضْحَكَالَا تَضْحَكَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)ضَحِكْتُمْتَضْحَكُونَاِضْحَكُوالَا تَضْحَكُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)ضَحِكْتُمَاتَضْحَكَانِاِضْحَكَالَا تَضْحَكَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)ضَحِكْتُنَّتَضْحَكْنَاِضْحَكْنَلَا تَضْحَكْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)ضَحِكَايَضْحَكَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)ضَحِكُوايَضْحَكُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)ضَحِكَتَاتَضْحَكَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)ضَحِكْنَيَضْحَكْنَ——

The classic ḥāl verb: جَاءَ ضَاحِكًا — “he came laughing.” Note the fatḥa on ضَاحِكًا; that is the ḥāl in naṣb, which is exactly what this chapter is about.

verb بَكَىٰ
maṣdar بُكَاء weeping
to weep
Form I · defective · يَبْكِي
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIبَكَيْتُأَبْكِي——
نَحْنُweبَكَيْنَانَبْكِي——
أَنْتَyou (m.)بَكَيْتَتَبْكِياِبْكِلَا تَبْكِ
أَنْتِyou (f.)بَكَيْتِتَبْكِينَاِبْكِيلَا تَبْكِي
هُوَheبَكَىٰيَبْكِي——
هِيَsheبَكَتْتَبْكِي——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)بَكَيْتُمَاتَبْكِيَانِاِبْكِيَالَا تَبْكِيَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)بَكَيْتُمْتَبْكُونَاِبْكُوالَا تَبْكُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)بَكَيْتُمَاتَبْكِيَانِاِبْكِيَالَا تَبْكِيَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)بَكَيْتُنَّتَبْكِينَاِبْكِينَلَا تَبْكِينَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)بَكَيَايَبْكِيَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)بَكَوْايَبْكُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)بَكَتَاتَبْكِيَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)بَكَيْنَيَبْكِينَ——

Defective, so the command loses its final letter: اِبْكِ. Its ḥāl form, بَاكِيًا, is the counterpart of ضَاحِكًا — and the two together give you the emotional range of a narration.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to comeجَاءَيَجِيءُمَجِيءجَاءٍ———جِئْ
to laughضَحِكَيَضْحَكُضَحِكضَاحِك———اِضْحَكْ
to weepبَكَىٰيَبْكِيبُكَاءبَاكٍ———اِبْكِ
One root through all eight moulds — جَاءَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ج ي ء, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. جَاءَ الْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ الْبَاطِلُ. — Truth came and falsehood vanished.

المضارع · present active. يَجِيءُ النَّصْرُ مَعَ الصَّبْرِ. — Victory comes with patience.

المصدر · the action itself. مَجِيءُ الرُّسُلِ رَحْمَةٌ لِلْعَالَمِينَ. — The coming of the messengers is a mercy to the worlds.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْجَائِي بِالْحَقِّ صَادِقٌ. — The one who comes with the truth is truthful.

الماضي المجهول · المضارع المجهول · اسم المفعول. No passive. جَاءَ takes no object — it is pure arrival — so it can never be turned around into a passive. The three empty cells are that rule, plainly shown.

الأمر · command. جِئْ بِالدَّلِيلِ عَلَىٰ مَا تَقُولُ. — Bring the evidence for what you say.

Where every ḥāl comes from

Look at the doer column: ضَاحِك · بَاكٍ · جَاءٍ. Put any of them into naṣb and you have a ḥāl.

You are not learning a new vocabulary of "state words." You are taking the فَاعِل mould you have owned since Day 2 and dropping it into a different slot. The mould is the same; only the case changes. This is what people mean when they call Arabic an economical language.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

جَاءَhe cameضَحِكَhe laughed بَكَىٰhe weptالحَالُthe state (ḥāl) التَّمْيِيزُspecificationقَائِمstanding جَالِسsittingصَامِتsilent وَحْدَهُaloneجَمِيعًاall together الْبَاطِلُfalsehoodشَهِيدwitness, martyr مُبَكِّرearlyاِزْدَادَhe increased كَفَىٰit sufficed

Spiral recap — Chapters 5, 12 & 16

صَالِحrighteousقَرِيبnear ثَلَاثَةthreeالسَّاعَةُthe hour أَنْto (+verb)أَرَادَhe wanted النِّيَّةُthe intentionالسَّبَبُthe cause
Before you close the book

"Naṣb is the house of the extras." Hold onto that. When you hear a fatḥa and there is no object in sight, you are not lost — you are standing in front of a ḥāl, a tamyīz, or an adverb, and the sentence is about to tell you how, when, or in what respect.

End of Chapter 18Next → Except, and Only
Day 19 · Grammar: exception & restriction

Except,
and Only

There is a sentence that a person says once and becomes a Muslim. Its whole force turns on two vowels — a fatḥa on one word and a ḍamma on another. Today you learn exactly why they are there, and why no other endings would do.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Parse لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ completely — every word, every vowel, and why.
  2. Understand لَا of the genus — a negation that wipes out an entire category, not just one instance.
  3. Use إِلَّا correctly in positive and negative sentences — where the case of the excepted noun changes.
  4. Master إِنَّمَا, the restriction particle, and see why adding مَا to إِنَّ disarms it.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01إِلَّا
except, but, other than
particle of exceptionTop 20
جَاءَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا.

The students came, except Zayd.

02إِنَّمَا
only, merely, nothing but
particle of restrictiondoes not cause naṣb
إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ.

Actions are only by intentions.

Note carefully: إِنَّ forces naṣb; إِنَّمَا does not. The little مَا — the grammarians call it مَا الكَافَّة, "the restraining mā" — physically restrains إِنَّ from governing.

03غَيْر
other than, not
noun of exceptionalways in an iḍāfa
غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ.

Not of those upon whom is wrath.

From the Fātiḥah. غَيْر is إِلَّا in noun form — and being a noun, it drags the word after it into jarr.

04شَهِدَ
he witnessed, he testified
verb · soundش – ه – دmaṣdar شَهَادَة
أَشْهَدُ أَنْ لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

I testify that there is no god but Allah.

05أَشْرَكَ
he associated partners (with Allah)
verb · Form IVش – ر – كmaṣdar شِرْك
لَا تُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ شَيْئًا.

Do not associate anything with Allah.

Form IV of شَرِكَ, to be a partner. Causative — so literally: to make something a partner. The doer-noun is مُشْرِك.

06الشَّهَادَةُ
the testimony; martyrdom
noun · maṣdarش – ه – د
الشَّهَادَتَانِ.

The two testimonies.

Note the dual — from Day 11. The two testimonies: of Allah, and of His Messenger.

07الْإِلٰهُ
the god, the deity
nounplural آلِهَة
لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

There is no god but Allah.

And the great etymological observation: اللهُ is, on the majority view, الْـ + إِلٰه — the God. The definite article is doing theology.

08الْحَوْلُ
power, capacity to change
nounح – و – ل
لَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ.

There is no power and no strength except by Allah.

09الْقُوَّةُ
strength
noun · fem.ق – و – ي
الْمُؤْمِنُ الْقَوِيُّ خَيْرٌ.

The strong believer is better.

10الْإِخْوَةُ
the brothers, brotherhood
noun · pluralأ – خ – و
إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ.

The believers are but brothers.

11الرَّحْمَةُ
mercy
nounDay 4, returning
وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ.

And We sent you not except as a mercy to the worlds.

12الْعَالَمُونَ
the worlds, all creatures
noun · sound pluralع – ل – م
رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ.

Lord of the worlds.

Same root as عِلْم. A عَالَم is that by which the Creator is known — a sign. The word for "world" is built on the word for "knowledge."

13الشِّرْكُ
associating partners with Allah
noun · maṣdarش – ر – ك
الشِّرْكُ ظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ.

Shirk is a tremendous wrong.

14الظُّلْمُ
wrongdoing, injustice, placing a thing wrongly
nounظ – ل – م
الظُّلْمُ ظُلُمَاتٌ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ.

Wrongdoing will be darknesses on the Day of Standing.

Same root as ظُلْمَة, darkness — from Day 9. Injustice and darkness are one word in Arabic, and the hadith puns on it deliberately.

15الْمُشْرِكُ
one who associates partners
doer of IVش – ر – ك
لَا يَدْخُلُ الْجَنَّةَ مُشْرِكٌ.

No one who associates partners enters Paradise.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

There is no god but Allah.

The most consequential sentence in the language, and its grammar is exact. لَا here does not deny a god — it denies the entire category. That is why إِلٰهَ is in naṣb and carries no tanwīn: it is lā of the genus, and it wipes out the class completely. Then إِلَّا اللهُ restores one — in rafʿ.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the entire genus · لَا النَّافِيَة لِلْجِنْس— إِلٰهَthe noun of لَا · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب إِلَّاthe exception particle · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— اللهُthe excepted — substituting for the omitted predicate · بَدَل مِنَ الخَبَرمَرْفُوع
02
إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِـ النِّيَّاتِ.

Actions are only by intentions.

Note what إِنَّمَا does not do: unlike إِنَّ, it leaves the following noun in rafʿ. Adding مَا neutralises إِنَّ's power to govern — and converts its meaning from emphasis to restriction.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّمَاrestriction — “only” · أَدَاة حَصْر— الْأَعْمَالُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع بِـpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— النِّيَّاتِobject of the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
03
أَشْهَدُ أَنْ لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

I testify that there is no god but Allah.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَشْهَدُverb, present, 1st person · فِعْل مُضَارِع— أَنْthe light أَنْ · حَرْف مَصْدَرِيّ— لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة— إِلٰهَthe noun of لَا · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— اللهُthe excepted · بَدَلمَرْفُوع
04
مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا طَالِبٌ.

No one came except a student.

The construction that trips everyone. When the sentence is negative and the thing excepted-from is omitted, إِلَّا stops governing and the noun simply takes whatever case its job demands. Here it is the doer — so rafʿ.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَاnegation · حَرْف نَفْي— جَاءَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— طَالِبٌthe doer — the exception is the subject · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع
05
جَاءَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا.

The students came, except Zayd.

Now compare with sentence 4. Here the sentence is positive and the group is stated — so إِلَّا does govern, and throws Zayd into naṣb. Positive → naṣb. Negative + omitted group → the noun keeps its own case. That is the whole rule.

Iʿrāb — word by word جَاءَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الطُّلَّابُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— زَيْدًاthe excepted one · مُسْتَثْنَىٰمَنْصُوب
06
وَ مَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ.

And We did not send you except as a mercy to the worlds.

A restriction, phrased as a double negative. Arabic could have said “We sent you as a mercy.” Instead it says “We sent you for nothing but mercy” — which is far stronger, and the strength is carried entirely by مَا ... إِلَّا.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَconjunction · حَرْف عَطْف— مَاnegation · حَرْف نَفْي— أَرْسَلْنَاكَverb + object pronoun · فِعْل وَفَاعِل وَمَفْعُول— إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— رَحْمَةًthe specifier of purpose · مَفْعُول لِأَجْلِهِمَنْصُوب لِلْعَالَمِينَafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
07
إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ.

The believers are but brothers.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّمَاrestriction — “only” · أَدَاة حَصْر— الْمُؤْمِنُونَthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع إِخْوَةٌwhat is said about them · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
08
لَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ.

There is no power and no strength except by Allah.

Two لَاs of the genus, side by side, and both nouns in naṣb without tanwīn. Once you can hear that pattern — a fatḥa with no -n — you will recognise لَا of the genus anywhere.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة— حَوْلَthe noun of لَا · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب وَلَاand no · — قُوَّةَthe noun of the second لَا · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— بِاللهِafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
09
مَا الْعِلْمُ إِلَّا نُورٌ.

Knowledge is nothing but a light.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَاnegation · حَرْف نَفْي— الْعِلْمُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— نُورٌthe predicate · خَبَرمَرْفُوع
10
غَيْرُ اللهِ.

Other than Allah.

غَيْر is إِلَّا in noun form. It takes whatever case إِلَّا's excepted noun would have taken, and the word after it goes into jarr as its muḍāf ilayh. Two ways to say the same thing — and the classical books use both.

Iʿrāb — word by word غَيْرُ“other than” — takes the excepted noun into jarr · مُسْتَثْنَىٰ بِـ غَيْرمَرْفُوع اللهِpossessed by غَيْر · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور

Section 4Grammar — The Shahādah, Parsed

Take the sentence apart slowly. Every vowel in it is doing work, and if any one of them changed, the meaning would collapse.

لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ

There is no god but Allah.

Step one: لَا of the genus

This is not the ordinary لَا that negates a verb. This is لَا النَّافِيَة لِلْجِنْس — the لَا that negates the entire genus.

The distinction matters enormously:

What it saysWhat it means
لَيْسَ إِلٰهٌ"There is not a god" — could be read as denying one particular god.
لَا إِلٰهَ"There is no such thing as a god" — the entire category is abolished, absolutely and without exception.

And لَا of the genus has a grammatical signature you can hear: its noun goes into naṣb, and it loses its tanwīn. Not ilāhan — just إِلٰهَ, ilāha. A bare fatḥa with no -n.

Hear it once and you will always hear it

لَا إِلٰهَ · لَا حَوْلَ · لَا قُوَّةَ · لَا رَيْبَ · لَا شَكَّ

Every one of them: لَا, then a bare fatḥa with no tanwīn. That is the sound of an absolute denial. Arabic has a dedicated grammatical construction whose only job is to say this thing does not exist, at all, anywhere — and the shahādah uses it.

Step two: the missing predicate

Ask a hard question: لَا إِلٰهَ is a subject with no predicate. No god ... is what?

The predicate has been deleted, and every grammarian agrees it is understood to be حَقٌّ — in truth, or worthy of worship. The full sense is: there is no god in truth except Allah.

This is not a pedantic point. It is the difference between denying that other gods exist — which is not what the sentence says, since people plainly do worship other things — and denying that any of them is rightfully a god. The deleted word is carrying the whole theology, and Arabic left it out on purpose, because the omission is more powerful than the statement.

Step three: إِلَّا اللهُ — and why it is in rafʿ

Here is the elegant part. Why does اللهُ take a ḍamma, when the exception in جَاءَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا takes a fatḥa?

Because the rule for إِلَّا depends on the sentence around it:

Sentence typeThe excepted noun takes...Example
Positive, group statednaṣb — إِلَّا governs itجَاءَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا زَيْدًا
Negative, group statedeither naṣb, or the case of the groupمَا جَاءَ الطُّلَّابُ إِلَّا زَيْدٌ
Negative, group omittedwhatever its job requiresمَا جَاءَ إِلَّا طَالِبٌ — rafʿ, because it is the doer

The shahādah is a negative sentence — لَا إِلٰهَ — so اللهُ is not governed by إِلَّا at all. It is a badal, a substitute, standing in for the deleted predicate. And so it takes rafʿ.

Why this is not a technicality

If اللهَ were in naṣb, the sentence would mean: "there is no god except Allah [is excluded from the denial]" — Allah would be an exception carved out of a denial.

Because it is in rafʿ, it instead restores what was denied: the category was emptied, and then He alone refills it. The ḍamma is not grammar decoration. It is the affirmation.

And إِنَّمَا: how adding mā disarms inna

You know from Day 6 that إِنَّ throws its noun into naṣb. So why does إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ leave الْأَعْمَالُ in rafʿ?

Because the مَا attached to it is مَا الكَافَّة — literally "the restraining mā." It physically prevents إِنَّ from governing anything. The particle is disarmed, and the sentence returns to an ordinary nominal sentence.

And in exchange for surrendering its grammatical power, إِنَّ gains a new meaning: only. This is a real trade, and Arabic makes it deliberately.

Section 5Reading Practice

كَلِمَةُ التَّوْحِيدِ

قَالَ الشَّيْخُ: هٰذِهِ كَلِمَةٌ قَصِيرَةٌ، وَلٰكِنَّ الْجِبَالَ لَا تَحْمِلُهَا.

«لَا إِلٰهَ» — نَفْيٌ. وَ«إِلَّا اللهُ» — إِثْبَاتٌ. فَالْكَلِمَةُ نَفْيٌ ثُمَّ إِثْبَاتٌ.

وَمَا نَفَعَتْ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنْ عَمِلَ بِهَا. لِأَنَّ إِبْلِيسَ يَعْرِفُهَا، وَمَا نَفَعَتْهُ.

وَإِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ. فَمَنْ قَالَهَا بِلِسَانِهِ وَخَالَفَهَا بِعَمَلِهِ، فَقَدْ نَقَضَهَا.

وَلَا حَوْلَ وَلَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللهِ.

The Word of Oneness

The shaykh said: this is a short word, but the mountains cannot carry it.

"There is no god" — a negation. And "except Allah" — an affirmation. So the word is a denial, and then an affirmation.

And it has benefited no one except the one who acted upon it. Because Iblīs knows it — and it did not benefit him.

And actions are only by intentions. So whoever says it with his tongue and contradicts it with his deeds has broken it.

And there is no power and no strength except by Allah.

New in context: الْجِبَال the mountains · تَحْمِلُ she carries · نَفْي negation · إِثْبَات affirmation · نَفَعَتْ it benefited · خَالَفَ he contradicted (III) · نَقَضَ he broke, undid

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

سَأَلَنِي طَالِبٌ: لِمَاذَا «إِلٰهَ» مَنْصُوبٌ، وَ«اللهُ» مَرْفُوعٌ؟ أَلَيْسَا فِي جُمْلَةٍ وَاحِدَةٍ؟

فَقُلْتُ: بَلَىٰ. وَلٰكِنَّ لِكُلِّ وَاحِدٍ مِنْهُمَا وَظِيفَةً غَيْرَ وَظِيفَةِ الْآخَرِ.

«إِلٰهَ» اسْمُ «لَا»، فَهُوَ مَنْصُوبٌ. وَ«اللهُ» بَدَلٌ مِنَ الْخَبَرِ الْمَحْذُوفِ، فَهُوَ مَرْفُوعٌ.

وَلَوْ نَصَبْتَ لَفْظَ الْجَلَالَةِ، لَأَخْرَجْتَهُ مِنَ الْإِثْبَاتِ إِلَى الِاسْتِثْنَاءِ. وَفَرْقٌ عَظِيمٌ بَيْنَهُمَا.

طَالِبٌ:

سُبْحَانَ اللهِ! فَتْحَةٌ وَضَمَّةٌ، وَفِيهِمَا كُلُّ هٰذَا؟

الشَّيْخُ:

نَعَمْ، يَا بُنَيَّ. وَلِهٰذَا دَرَسْنَا الْإِعْرَابَ.

Shaykh: A student asked me: why is ilāha in naṣb, and Allāhu in rafʿ? Are they not in one sentence? So I said: yes indeed. But each of them has a function other than the function of the other. Ilāha is the noun of لَا, so it is in naṣb. And Allāhu is a substitute for the deleted predicate, so it is in rafʿ. And had you put the Divine Name into naṣb, you would have taken it out of affirmation and into exception. And there is a tremendous difference between the two.

Student: Glory be to Allah! A fatḥa and a ḍamma — and all of this is in them?

Shaykh: Yes, my son. And this is why we studied iʿrāb.

New: بَلَىٰ yes indeed · وَظِيفَة a function · لَفْظ الْجَلَالَة the Divine Name · فَرْق a difference

Say it yourself

Exception, spoken. The first one you already say every day — now you know its grammar.

لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

مَا جَاءَ إِلَّا طَالِبٌ وَاحِدٌ.

لَا أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْحَقَّ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb شَهِدَ
maṣdar شَهَادَة testimony, witnessing
to witness, to testify
Form I · sound · يَشْهَدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIشَهِدْتُأَشْهَدُ——
نَحْنُweشَهِدْنَانَشْهَدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)شَهِدْتَتَشْهَدُاِشْهَدْلَا تَشْهَدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)شَهِدْتِتَشْهَدِينَاِشْهَدِيلَا تَشْهَدِي
هُوَheشَهِدَيَشْهَدُ——
هِيَsheشَهِدَتْتَشْهَدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)شَهِدْتُمَاتَشْهَدَانِاِشْهَدَالَا تَشْهَدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)شَهِدْتُمْتَشْهَدُونَاِشْهَدُوالَا تَشْهَدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)شَهِدْتُمَاتَشْهَدَانِاِشْهَدَالَا تَشْهَدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)شَهِدْتُنَّتَشْهَدْنَاِشْهَدْنَلَا تَشْهَدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)شَهِدَايَشْهَدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)شَهِدُوايَشْهَدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)شَهِدَتَاتَشْهَدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)شَهِدْنَيَشْهَدْنَ——

أَشْهَدُ أَنْ لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ — the أَنَا row of this grid, present tense, and the sentence a person utters to enter Islam. Its maṣdar شَهَادَة means both testimony and martyrdom — the martyr, شَهِيد, is one who bore witness with his life.

verb أَشْرَكَ
maṣdar إِشْرَاك · شِرْك associating partners
to associate partners (with Allah)
Form IV · يُشْرِكُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIأَشْرَكْتُأُشْرِكُ——
نَحْنُweأَشْرَكْنَانُشْرِكُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)أَشْرَكْتَتُشْرِكُأَشْرِكْلَا تُشْرِكْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)أَشْرَكْتِتُشْرِكِينَأَشْرِكِيلَا تُشْرِكِي
هُوَheأَشْرَكَيُشْرِكُ——
هِيَsheأَشْرَكَتْتُشْرِكُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)أَشْرَكْتُمَاتُشْرِكَانِأَشْرِكَالَا تُشْرِكَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)أَشْرَكْتُمْتُشْرِكُونَأَشْرِكُوالَا تُشْرِكُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)أَشْرَكْتُمَاتُشْرِكَانِأَشْرِكَالَا تُشْرِكَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)أَشْرَكْتُنَّتُشْرِكْنَأَشْرِكْنَلَا تُشْرِكْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)أَشْرَكَايُشْرِكَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)أَشْرَكُوايُشْرِكُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)أَشْرَكَتَاتُشْرِكَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)أَشْرَكْنَيُشْرِكْنَ——

Form IV of شَرِكَ (to share, to be a partner). Form IV is causative — so أَشْرَكَ is literally to make [something] a partner. The gravest sin in Islam is named by a grammatical mould, and its doer-noun is مُشْرِك.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to witnessشَهِدَيَشْهَدُشَهَادَةشَاهِدشُهِدَيُشْهَدُمَشْهُوداِشْهَدْ
to associate (IV)أَشْرَكَيُشْرِكُإِشْرَاكمُشْرِكأُشْرِكَيُشْرَكُمُشْرَكأَشْرِكْ
One root through all eight moulds — شَهِدَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ش ه د, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. شَهِدَ أَنْ لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ. — He testified that there is no god but Allah.

المضارع · present active. يَشْهَدُ الْمُؤْمِنُ بِالْحَقِّ. — The believer testifies to the truth.

المصدر · the action itself. الشَّهَادَةُ رُكْنُ الْإِسْلَامِ الْأَوَّلُ. — The testimony is the first pillar of Islam.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. كَفَىٰ بِاللهِ شَاهِدًا. — Allah suffices as a witness.

الماضي المجهول · past passive. شُهِدَ لَهُ بِالصِّدْقِ. — He was testified to be truthful.

المضارع المجهول · present passive. يُشْهَدُ لِلْعُلَمَاءِ بِالْفَضْلِ. — The scholars are attested to have merit.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. ذٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَشْهُودٌ. — That is a day that is witnessed.

الأمر · command. اِشْهَدْ بِالْحَقِّ وَلَوْ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِكَ. — Bear witness to the truth, even against yourself.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

إِلَّاexceptإِنَّمَاonly غَيْرother thanشَهِدَhe testified أَشْرَكَhe associated partnersالشَّهَادَةُtestimony الْإِلٰهُthe godالْحَوْلُpower الْقُوَّةُstrengthالْإِخْوَةُbrothers الرَّحْمَةُmercyالْعَالَمُونَthe worlds الشِّرْكُshirkالظُّلْمُwrongdoing الْمُشْرِكُone who associates

Spiral recap — Chapters 6, 15 & 17

إِنَّindeedلٰكِنَّbut الْإِيمَانُfaithتَابَhe repented غَفَرَhe forgaveالْمَجْهُولُthe passive قِيلَit was saidمَكْتُوبwritten, decreed
Before you close the book

You have just parsed the most important sentence in your religion — not devotionally, but grammatically. And the grammar turned out to say exactly what the theology says: the category is emptied, and then He alone refills it.

The student in the dialogue asked the right question. A fatḥa and a ḍamma — and all of this is in them? Yes. And that is why we studied iʿrāb.

End of Chapter 19Next → Emphasis and Oaths
Day 20 · Grammar: emphasis & oaths

How Arabic
Raises Its Voice

English emphasises with adverbs — really, truly, certainly — or with italics, or by shouting. Arabic emphasises structurally: it stacks particles, doubles letters, swears oaths, and inverts word order. And it can pile three or four of these on a single sentence.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Recognise the five emphasis devices: إِنَّ, the lām, قَدْ, the heavy nūn, and the oath.
  2. Understand why قَدْ means certainly with a past verb and perhaps with a present one.
  3. Parse a full Qur'anic oath — the وَ, the noun in jarr, and the answer that follows.
  4. Hear word order as emphasis: why إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا hits harder than the same words rearranged.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01قَدْ
indeed, already (+past) · perhaps, sometimes (+present)
particletwo opposite meanings
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ.

The believers have indeed succeeded.

Everything depends on the verb after it. قَدْ + past = certainty. قَدْ + present = possibility. One particle, two opposite jobs — and only the tense tells you which.

02لَـ
truly, certainly (emphatic lām)
prefixattaches to the predicate
إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ.

Indeed, Allah is truly Oft-Forgiving, Merciful.

Do not confuse it with the لِـ of purpose (Day 16, kasra) or the لِـ meaning "for" (Day 4). The emphatic lām carries a fatḥa, and it appears after إِنَّ or in the answer to an oath.

03النُّونُ الثَّقِيلَةُ
the heavy nūn — ـَنَّ
suffix of emphasis
لَأَجْتَهِدَنَّ.

I shall certainly strive.

A doubled نّ welded onto the end of a verb, and it means: absolutely, without fail. It also freezes the verb — it becomes mabnī and stops taking case endings altogether.

04حَلَفَ
he swore an oath
verb · soundح – ل – ف
حَلَفَ بِاللهِ.

He swore by Allah.

05أَقْسَمَ
he swore (solemnly)
verb · Form IVق – س – مmaṣdar قَسَم
لَا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ.

I swear by the Day of Standing.

Note the لَا: in classical oath language it is emphatic, not negative — a famous crux which the commentators discuss at length. Do not translate it as "I do not swear."

06أَكَّدَ
he confirmed, he emphasised
verb · Form IIأ – ك – دmaṣdar تَأْكِيد
أَكَّدَ الشَّيْخُ هٰذِهِ الْمَسْأَلَةَ.

The shaykh emphasised this issue.

07الْقَسَمُ
the oath
noun · maṣdarق – س – م
جَوَابُ الْقَسَمِ.

The answer to the oath.

08أَفْلَحَ
he succeeded, he prospered
verb · Form IVف – ل – ح
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاهَا.

He has succeeded who purifies it.

The root originally means to plough, to split the earth — a farmer is a فَلَّاح. To succeed, in Arabic, is to break open the ground and let something grow. The call to prayer says حَيَّ عَلَى الْفَلَاحِ: come to the harvest.

09الْعُسْرُ — الْيُسْرُ
hardship — ease
nouns · a pair
إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا.

Indeed, with hardship comes ease.

10الْعَصْرُ
time, the age; the afternoon
nounع – ص – ر
وَالْعَصْرِ. إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ.

By the passage of time. Indeed, mankind is surely in loss.

11الْخُسْرُ
loss, ruin
nounخ – س – ر
إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ.

Indeed, mankind is surely in loss.

12الْإِنْسَانُ
the human being, mankind
nounأ – ن – س
خُلِقَ الْإِنْسَانُ ضَعِيفًا.

Man was created weak.

Note ضَعِيفًا — a ḥāl from yesterday, in naṣb. And خُلِقَ — a passive from Day 17. Two chapters ago these were new; now they are furniture.

13أَحَبَّ
he loved
verb · Form IVح – ب – بmaṣdar حُبّ
إِنِّي أُحِبُّكَ فِي اللهِ.

I truly love you for the sake of Allah.

14الصَّلَاةُ
the prayer
noun · fem.plural صَلَوَات
قَدْ قَامَتِ الصَّلَاةُ.

The prayer has indeed begun.

15التَّوْكِيدُ
emphasis, corroboration
grammar termو – ك – د
هٰذِهِ لَامُ التَّوْكِيدِ.

This is the lām of emphasis.

Section 3Ten Common Sentences — with Full Iʿrāb

01
إِنَّ اللهَ لَـ غَفُورٌ.

Indeed, Allah is truly Oft-Forgiving.

Two emphasisers stacked. إِنَّ at the front, then لَـ on the predicate. English can only manage this with an adverb — truly, certainly — but Arabic does it structurally, and the effect is far heavier.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis — “indeed” · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— اللهَthe noun of إِنَّ · اِسْم إِنَّمَنْصُوب لَـthe lām of emphasis · لَام الِابْتِدَاء— غَفُورٌthe predicate · خَبَر إِنَّمَرْفُوع
02
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ.

The believers have indeed succeeded.

قَدْ + past = certainty. But قَدْ + present means the opposite — “perhaps, sometimes.” Same particle, two tenses, two opposite meanings. Listen for the verb that follows it.

Iʿrāb — word by word قَدْemphasis on a past verb — “indeed, already” · حَرْف تَحْقِيق— أَفْلَحَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الْمُؤْمِنُونَthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع
03
وَ اللهِ لَـ أَجْتَهِدَنَّ.

By Allah, I shall certainly strive.

The full oath machinery. A وَ that means “by”; a noun in jarr; then the answer, carrying both a لَـ and the doubled نَّ. Three separate emphasisers on one sentence — this is the strongest thing Arabic can say.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَthe wāw of oath · وَاو القَسَم— اللهِthe one sworn by · مُقْسَم بِهِمَجْرُور لَـemphasis on the answer · لَام جَوَاب القَسَم— أَجْتَهِدَنَّverb + heavy nūn of emphasis · فِعْل مُضَارِع مُؤَكَّدمَبْنِيّ
04
وَ الْعَصْرِ.

By the passage of time.

The opening of Sūrat al-ʿAṣr. When you hear a وَ followed by a noun in jarr at the start of a sūrah, it is an oath — and the answer to it is coming.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَthe wāw of oath · وَاو القَسَم— الْعَصْرِthe one sworn by · مُقْسَم بِهِمَجْرُور
05
إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَـ فِي خُسْرٍ.

Indeed, mankind is surely in loss.

The answer to that oath. Note that the whole verse is one long emphasis: the oath, then إِنَّ, then لَـ. Three layers, before the sentence has even made its claim.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— الْإِنْسَانَthe noun of إِنَّ · اِسْم إِنَّمَنْصُوب لَـemphasis · لَام الِابْتِدَاء— فِيpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— خُسْرٍafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
06
لَا تَنْسَيَنَّ الدُّعَاءَ.

Do not ever forget the supplication.

The heavy nūn on a prohibition turns “do not forget” into “do not ever forget.” It is the difference between advice and insistence.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاprohibition · لَا النَّاهِيَة— تَنْسَيَنَّverb + heavy nūn · فِعْل مُضَارِع مُؤَكَّدمَبْنِيّ الدُّعَاءَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
07
أَقْسَمَ بِـ اللهِ.

He swore by Allah.

Iʿrāb — word by word أَقْسَمَverb, past, Form IV · فِعْل مَاضٍ— بِـpreposition of the oath · حَرْف جَرّ— اللهِthe one sworn by · مُقْسَم بِهِمَجْرُور
08
إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا.

Indeed, with hardship comes ease.

Word order as emphasis. The predicate has been pulled to the front, ahead of the subject — which in Arabic foregrounds it. The verse does not say “ease is with hardship.” It says: with hardship — and only then tells you what.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— مَعَadverb of place, fronted · ظَرْف مَكَانمَنْصُوب الْعُسْرِpossessed by the adverb · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور يُسْرًاthe noun of إِنَّ, delayed · اِسْم إِنَّ مُؤَخَّرمَنْصُوب
09
وَاللهِ إِنِّي أُحِبُّكَ فِي اللهِ.

By Allah, I truly love you for the sake of Allah.

A hadith, and one of the warmest sentences in the tradition. Note the emphasis is not decorative — the Prophet ﷺ instructed that this be said aloud to the person concerned.

Iʿrāb — word by word وَاللهِoath, contracted · وَاو القَسَم— إِنِّيإِنَّ + attached pronoun · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— أُحِبُّكَverb + object pronoun · فِعْل مُضَارِع— فِيpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— اللهِafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
10
قَدْ قَامَتِ الصَّلَاةُ.

The prayer has indeed begun.

Said in the iqāmah, twice, every day. قَدْ + a past verb: the thing has definitely happened. That is why the congregation stands.

Iʿrāb — word by word قَدْemphasis · حَرْف تَحْقِيق— قَامَتِverb, past, feminine · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الصَّلَاةُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع

Section 4Grammar — Five Ways to Insist

DeviceWhere it sitsForce
إِنَّat the head of a nominal sentence"indeed" — and it throws the noun into naṣb
لَـ — the emphatic lāmon the predicate, after إِنَّ"truly, certainly"
قَدْbefore a verbpast → certainty; present → possibility
ـَنَّ — the heavy nūnwelded to the end of a verb"absolutely, without fail" — and freezes the verb
the oathat the front of everythingthe strongest device Arabic possesses

They stack — and that is the point

Look at what Sūrat al-ʿAṣr does before it has told you anything at all:

وَالْعَصْرِ. إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَـفِي خُسْرٍ.

By the passage of time. Indeed, mankind is surely in loss.

An oath. Then إِنَّ. Then the emphatic لَـ. Three separate devices of emphasis, one after another, before the claim even lands.

English simply cannot do this. A translator gets "Indeed, mankind is surely in loss" — two words of emphasis where Arabic has three structural layers, and the oath is left dangling as an odd opening phrase. The force is untranslatable, and that is a real reason to learn the language rather than read it in translation.

The oath, in three parts

PartArabicGrammar
the swearing particleوَ · بِـ · تَـa preposition — so the next word is in jarr
the thing sworn byاللهِ · الْعَصْرِمُقْسَم بِهِ — in jarr
the answerلَـ... · إِنَّ...جَوَاب الْقَسَم — usually carries لَـ or إِنَّ
The listening cue

When a sūrah opens with وَ + a noun in jarr — وَالْعَصْرِ, وَالشَّمْسِ, وَالضُّحَىٰ, وَالتِّينِ — that is not the ordinary "and." It is an oath.

And an oath creates a debt: the answer must come. So when you hear one, hold the sentence open in your mind and wait. Everything until the إِنَّ or the لَـ is preamble; the payload arrives after.

Word order as emphasis

One last device, and the subtlest. Arabic can front a word — move it ahead of where it grammatically belongs — and that fronting is the emphasis.

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ

"You alone we worship." — the object has been dragged to the very front.

The neutral order would be نَعْبُدُكَ — "we worship You." By pulling the object to the front, the verse changes the meaning to You, and no one else. The exclusivity is not in any word. It is in the position.

Same with إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا: the predicate has been fronted ahead of the subject, so the emphasis falls on with hardship — the ease is bound to it, inseparable from it. Rearranged into ordinary order, the verse would say something considerably weaker.

Section 5Reading Practice

سُورَةُ الْعَصْرِ

وَالْعَصْرِ. إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ. إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ.

قَالَ الشَّافِعِيُّ: لَوْ مَا أَنْزَلَ اللهُ حُجَّةً عَلَىٰ خَلْقِهِ إِلَّا هٰذِهِ السُّورَةَ، لَكَفَتْهُمْ.

اُنْظُرْ إِلَى التَّوْكِيدِ: قَسَمٌ، ثُمَّ «إِنَّ»، ثُمَّ اللَّامُ. ثَلَاثَةُ تَوْكِيدَاتٍ قَبْلَ الْخَبَرِ.

وَلِمَاذَا؟ لِأَنَّ الْخَبَرَ ثَقِيلٌ: كُلُّ إِنْسَانٍ فِي خُسْرٍ. وَلَا يُقَالُ مِثْلُ هٰذَا إِلَّا بِتَوْكِيدٍ.

Sūrat al-ʿAṣr

By the passage of time. Indeed, mankind is surely in loss — except those who believed, and did righteous deeds, and enjoined one another to truth, and enjoined one another to patience.

Al-Shāfiʿī said: had Allah sent down no proof upon His creation but this sūrah, it would have sufficed them.

Look at the emphasis: an oath, then inna, then the lām. Three emphasisers before the predicate.

And why? Because the predicate is heavy: every human being is in loss. And a thing like that is not said except with emphasis.

New in context: تَوَاصَوْا they enjoined one another (VI) · حُجَّة a proof · خَلْق creation · لَكَفَتْهُمْ it would have sufficed them · ثَقِيل heavy

Everything you own, in three verses

An oath (today) · إِنَّ (Day 6) · the emphatic lām (today) · إِلَّا (Day 19) · a relative clause with الَّذِينَ (Day 8) · Form VI تَوَاصَوْا (Day 10) · two prepositional phrases in jarr (Day 4).

Sūrat al-ʿAṣr is three verses long, and it uses seven of your chapters. That is not a coincidence — it is what happens when a language is used at full power in a very small space.

Section 6Listening Practice

الشَّيْخُ:

لَاحِظُوا: الْعَرَبِيَّةُ لَا تُؤَكِّدُ بِالصَّوْتِ. تُؤَكِّدُ بِالْبِنَاءِ.

فَإِذَا أَرَادَ الْمُتَكَلِّمُ أَنْ يَشْتَدَّ، لَمْ يَرْفَعْ صَوْتَهُ. بَلْ زَادَ حَرْفًا.

«اللهُ غَفُورٌ» — خَبَرٌ. «إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ» — تَوْكِيدٌ. «إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَفُورٌ» — تَوْكِيدَانِ.

وَاللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَفُورٌ — ثَلَاثَةٌ. وَكُلَّمَا زَادَ الْحَرْفُ، اشْتَدَّ الْمَعْنَىٰ.

طَالِبٌ:

يَا شَيْخُ، فَهَلْ نَقُولُ ذٰلِكَ فِي كَلَامِنَا؟

الشَّيْخُ:

قُلْهُ إِذَا صَدَقْتَ. وَأَمَّا مَنْ يُكْثِرُ الْحَلِفَ، فَقَدْ نَقَصَ صِدْقُهُ. التَّوْكِيدُ كَالْمِلْحِ.

Shaykh: Notice: Arabic does not emphasise with the voice. It emphasises with the structure. So when a speaker wants to press harder, he does not raise his voice — he adds a letter. "Allah is Forgiving" — a statement. "Indeed Allah is Forgiving" — one emphasis. "Indeed Allah is truly Forgiving" — two. "By Allah, indeed Allah is truly Forgiving" — three. And every time a letter is added, the meaning intensifies.

Student: Shaykh — so should we say that in our own speech?

Shaykh: Say it when you are telling the truth. As for the one who swears often — his truthfulness has diminished. Emphasis is like salt.

New: لَاحِظُوا notice · الْبِنَاء the structure · يَشْتَدَّ he presses harder · كُلَّمَا every time that · يُكْثِرُ he does much of · نَقَصَ it diminished · الْمِلْح the salt

Say it yourself

Three ways to insist. Say them with the weight they deserve.

إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ.

إِنَّمَا الْأَعْمَالُ بِالنِّيَّاتِ.

وَاللهِ لَأَطْلُبَنَّ الْعِلْمَ.

Section 7Word Sheets

verb حَلَفَ
maṣdar حَلِف swearing
to swear an oath
Form I · sound · يَحْلِفُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIحَلَفْتُأَحْلِفُ——
نَحْنُweحَلَفْنَانَحْلِفُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)حَلَفْتَتَحْلِفُاِحْلِفْلَا تَحْلِفْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)حَلَفْتِتَحْلِفِينَاِحْلِفِيلَا تَحْلِفِي
هُوَheحَلَفَيَحْلِفُ——
هِيَsheحَلَفَتْتَحْلِفُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)حَلَفْتُمَاتَحْلِفَانِاِحْلِفَالَا تَحْلِفَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)حَلَفْتُمْتَحْلِفُونَاِحْلِفُوالَا تَحْلِفُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)حَلَفْتُمَاتَحْلِفَانِاِحْلِفَالَا تَحْلِفَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)حَلَفْتُنَّتَحْلِفْنَاِحْلِفْنَلَا تَحْلِفْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)حَلَفَايَحْلِفَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)حَلَفُوايَحْلِفُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)حَلَفَتَاتَحْلِفَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)حَلَفْنَيَحْلِفْنَ——

The everyday verb for swearing. Distinguish it from أَقْسَمَ below, which is the formal, Qur'anic register — the difference is roughly that between “I swear” and “I do solemnly attest.”

verb أَقْسَمَ
maṣdar قَسَم an oath
to swear (solemnly)
Form IV · يُقْسِمُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIأَقْسَمْتُأُقْسِمُ——
نَحْنُweأَقْسَمْنَانُقْسِمُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)أَقْسَمْتَتُقْسِمُأَقْسِمْلَا تُقْسِمْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)أَقْسَمْتِتُقْسِمِينَأَقْسِمِيلَا تُقْسِمِي
هُوَheأَقْسَمَيُقْسِمُ——
هِيَsheأَقْسَمَتْتُقْسِمُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)أَقْسَمْتُمَاتُقْسِمَانِأَقْسِمَالَا تُقْسِمَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)أَقْسَمْتُمْتُقْسِمُونَأَقْسِمُوالَا تُقْسِمُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)أَقْسَمْتُمَاتُقْسِمَانِأَقْسِمَالَا تُقْسِمَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)أَقْسَمْتُنَّتُقْسِمْنَأَقْسِمْنَلَا تُقْسِمْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)أَقْسَمَايُقْسِمَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)أَقْسَمُوايُقْسِمُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)أَقْسَمَتَاتُقْسِمَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)أَقْسَمْنَيُقْسِمْنَ——

The verb behind every Qur'anic oath. لَا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ — and note that the classical لَا there is emphatic, not negative: a famous crux for commentators.

verb أَكَّدَ
maṣdar تَأْكِيد emphasis, confirmation
to confirm, to emphasise
Form II · يُؤَكِّدُ
الماضيpastالمضارعpresentالأمرcommandالنهيprohibition
أَنَاIأَكَّدْتُأُؤَكِّدُ——
نَحْنُweأَكَّدْنَانُؤَكِّدُ——
أَنْتَyou (m.)أَكَّدْتَتُؤَكِّدُأَكِّدْلَا تُؤَكِّدْ
أَنْتِyou (f.)أَكَّدْتِتُؤَكِّدِينَأَكِّدِيلَا تُؤَكِّدِي
هُوَheأَكَّدَيُؤَكِّدُ——
هِيَsheأَكَّدَتْتُؤَكِّدُ——
أَنْتُمَاyou two (m.)أَكَّدْتُمَاتُؤَكِّدَانِأَكِّدَالَا تُؤَكِّدَا
أَنْتُمْyou (m. pl.)أَكَّدْتُمْتُؤَكِّدُونَأَكِّدُوالَا تُؤَكِّدُوا
أَنْتُمَاyou two (f.)أَكَّدْتُمَاتُؤَكِّدَانِأَكِّدَالَا تُؤَكِّدَا
أَنْتُنَّyou (f. pl.)أَكَّدْتُنَّتُؤَكِّدْنَأَكِّدْنَلَا تُؤَكِّدْنَ
هُمَاthey two (m.)أَكَّدَايُؤَكِّدَانِ——
هُمْthey (m.)أَكَّدُوايُؤَكِّدُونَ——
هُمَاthey two (f.)أَكَّدَتَاتُؤَكِّدَانِ——
هُنَّthey (f.)أَكَّدْنَيُؤَكِّدْنَ——

The grammarians' own word for what this chapter studies: التَّأْكِيد, emphasis. Form II — causative — so literally to make [something] firm.

Section 8Derivation Table

meaning الماضيpast · activeالمضارعpresent · active المصدرthe action itselfاسم الفاعلthe doer الماضي المجهولpast · passiveالمضارع المجهولpresent · passive اسم المفعولthe done-toالأمرcommand
to swearحَلَفَيَحْلِفُحَلِفحَالِف——مَحْلُوفاِحْلِفْ
to swear (IV)أَقْسَمَيُقْسِمُقَسَممُقْسِم——مُقْسَمأَقْسِمْ
to emphasise (II)أَكَّدَيُؤَكِّدُتَأْكِيدمُؤَكِّدأُكِّدَيُؤَكَّدُمُؤَكَّدأَكِّدْ
One root through all eight moulds — حَلَفَ

The table gives the forms. Here is each mould living in a sentence — one root ح ل ف, eight different jobs.

الماضي · past active. حَلَفَ بِاللهِ لَيَصْدُقَنَّ. — He swore by Allah that he would be truthful.

المضارع · present active. لَا يَحْلِفُ الْمُؤْمِنُ كَاذِبًا. — The believer does not swear falsely.

المصدر · the action itself. الْحَلِفُ بِغَيْرِ اللهِ لَا يَجُوزُ. — Swearing by other than Allah is not permitted.

اسم الفاعل · the doer. الْحَالِفُ صَادِقًا لَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ. — One who swears truthfully bears no sin.

الماضي المجهول · المضارع المجهول. No passive verb in use — Arabic does not say ‘it was sworn.’ These two verb-cells stay empty, even though the done-to noun below still exists.

اسم المفعول · the done-to. الْمَحْلُوفُ عَلَيْهِ يَمِينٌ لَازِمَةٌ. — That which is sworn upon is a binding oath.

الأمر · command. اِحْلِفْ بِاللهِ إِنْ كُنْتَ صَادِقًا. — Swear by Allah if you are truthful.

Section 9Review

Today's fifteen

قَدْindeed / perhapsلَـtruly (emphatic lām) النُّونُ الثَّقِيلَةُthe heavy nūnحَلَفَhe swore أَقْسَمَhe swore (IV)أَكَّدَhe emphasised (II) الْقَسَمُthe oathأَفْلَحَhe succeeded الْعُسْرُhardshipالْيُسْرُease الْعَصْرُtime, the ageالْخُسْرُloss الْإِنْسَانُmankindأَحَبَّhe loved التَّوْكِيدُemphasis

Spiral recap — Chapters 6, 16 & 18

إِنَّindeedلَعَلَّperhaps أَنْto (+verb)كَيْso that النِّيَّةُthe intentionالحَالُthe state (ḥāl) التَّمْيِيزُspecificationوَحْدَهُalone
Before you close the book

"Arabic does not emphasise with the voice. It emphasises with the structure."

That single observation explains why a Qur'anic verse can feel so much heavier than its translation, and why reciters do not need to shout. The insistence is already inside the grammar — and you can now hear it.

End of Chapter 20Next → Recap II, and the Registers Ahead
Day 21 · Consolidation — no new grammar

The Grammar
Is Finished

Twenty days. Today there is nothing new — and that sentence deserves a pause, because it means something. There is no longer a structure in Arabic that you have not met. What remains is not harder. It is only more.

Section 1The Complete Grammar, on One Page

ChWhat it gave youHow you hear it
1Nominal sentenceTwo nouns, no verb. Definite + indefinite = a claim.
★Iʿrābḍamma = doer · fatḥa = object & extras · kasra = preposition or possession
2Verbal sentence, pastVerb first. Six tails.
3Present tenseThe prefixes أَنَيْتُ.
4Iḍāfa & attached pronounsFirst noun bare; second in jarr.
5AdjectivesFollow in four ways at once.
6Sisters of كَانَ / إِنَّكَانَ lowers the second; إِنَّ lowers the first.
7Negation & questionsلَا · مَا · لَمْ · لَنْ — chosen by time.
8Relative clausesDefinite noun → الَّذِي + a returner pronoun.
9–10The ten mouldsḌamma on the prefix = derived. اِسْتَـ = seeking.
11Plurals-ūna doer · -īna not. Broken plurals: learn in pairs.
12Numbers3–10 reverse gender; the counted noun is an iḍāfa.
13Weak verbsA weak letter gives way rather than carry a sukūn.
15Conditionalsإِذَا certain · إِنْ possible · لَوْ impossible.
16The subjunctiveأَنْ · لَنْ · كَيْ · لِـ · حَتَّىٰ → a fatḥa on the verb.
17The passiveu–i in the past; u–a in the present.
18Ḥāl & tamyīzNaṣb is the house of the extras.
19Exception & restrictionلَا of the genus: bare fatḥa, no tanwīn.
20Emphasis & oathsArabic emphasises with structure, not volume.
What is actually left

Read that table honestly. Every construction in it appears on every page of the Qur'an, of Bukhārī, of any book of fiqh, and in every lecture you will ever listen to. There is nothing structural left to learn.

The remaining nine chapters are not more grammar. They are register — how each of the four worlds you want to enter actually uses this machinery, and what its habits and formulae are. And then vocabulary, which is not a difficulty. It is a schedule.

Section 2Ten Sentences That Use Everything

These are the ten you should be able to parse cold. Cover the tables and try each one first.

01
وَ الْعَصْرِ.

By the passage of time.

Ch 20. A وَ followed by jarr at the head of a sūrah is an oath, not an “and.”

Iʿrāb — word by word وَthe wāw of oath · وَاو القَسَم— الْعَصْرِthe one sworn by · مُقْسَم بِهِمَجْرُور
02
إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَـ فِي خُسْرٍ.

Indeed, mankind is surely in loss.

Ch 6 + Ch 20. إِنَّ lowers the first; the lām then piles on top.

Iʿrāb — word by word إِنَّemphasis · حَرْف تَوْكِيد— الْإِنْسَانَthe noun of inna · اِسْم إِنَّمَنْصُوب لَـemphatic lām · لَام الِابْتِدَاء— فِيpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— خُسْرٍafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
03
إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ.

Except those who believed and did righteous deeds.

Ch 8 + Ch 19. The relative pronoun is mabnī — frozen — so it cannot show a fatḥa. Grammarians say it is in the position of naṣb: مَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا. Note also الصَّالِحَاتِ: a feminine plural object, and feminine plurals take a kasra even in naṣb (Ch 11).

Iʿrāb — word by word إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— الَّذِينَthe excepted ones · مُسْتَثْنَىٰمَنْصُوب مَحَلًّا آمَنُواverb, past, Form IV · فِعْل مَاضٍ— وَعَمِلُواand did · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الصَّالِحَاتِthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
04
اِهْدِ نَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ.

Guide us to the straight path.

Ch 5 + Ch 13. The adjective follows its noun into naṣb — that is the fourth agreement. And the verb has lost its final ي because a command is jazm and a weak letter cannot carry a sukūn.

Iʿrāb — word by word اِهْدِcommand, defective — final letter deleted · فِعْل أَمْرمَجْزُوم نَاobject pronoun · ضَمِير مُتَّصِل— الصِّرَاطَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب الْمُسْتَقِيمَits adjective, following it · نَعْتمَنْصُوب
05
مَنْ سَلَكَ طَرِيقًا يَلْتَمِسُ فِيهِ عِلْمًا.

Whoever travels a road seeking knowledge upon it...

Ch 8 + Ch 15. Note that طَرِيقًا is indefinite, so the clause describing it needs no الَّذِي at all — Arabic simply attaches the verb.

Iʿrāb — word by word مَنْconditional — “whoever” · اِسْم شَرْط— سَلَكَverb of the condition · فِعْل الشَّرْط— طَرِيقًاthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب يَلْتَمِسُForm VIII, describing the road · فِعْل مُضَارِع— فِيهِpreposition + pronoun · جَارّ وَمَجْرُورمَجْرُور عِلْمًاthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
06
بُنِيَ الْإِسْلَامُ عَلَىٰ خَمْسٍ.

Islam was built upon five.

Ch 17. Hear the u–i: buniya. And notice the object has been promoted into rafʿ to fill the empty subject slot.

Iʿrāb — word by word بُنِيَpassive verb — the doer is hidden · فِعْل مَبْنِيّ لِلْمَجْهُول— الْإِسْلَامُthe deputy of the doer · نَائِب الفَاعِلمَرْفُوع عَلَىٰpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— خَمْسٍafter the preposition · اِسْم مَجْرُورمَجْرُور
07
جَاءَ الطَّالِبُ ضَاحِكًا.

The student came laughing.

Ch 18. Indefinite, in naṣb, owner definite — that is a ḥāl every time.

Iʿrāb — word by word جَاءَverb, past · فِعْل مَاضٍ— الطَّالِبُthe doer · فَاعِلمَرْفُوع ضَاحِكًاthe circumstance · حَالمَنْصُوب
08
أُرِيدُ أَنْ أَتَعَلَّمَ الْعَرَبِيَّةَ.

I want to learn Arabic.

Ch 16. Arabic has no infinitive — so أَنْ plus a fully conjugated verb in naṣb does the work of English to learn.

Iʿrāb — word by word أُرِيدُForm IV, present · فِعْل مُضَارِع— أَنْthe subjunctive particle · حَرْف نَصْب— أَتَعَلَّمَForm V, in naṣb · فِعْل مُضَارِع مَنْصُوبمَنْصُوب الْعَرَبِيَّةَthe object · مَفْعُول بِهِمَنْصُوب
09
لَا إِلٰهَ إِلَّا اللهُ.

There is no god but Allah.

Ch 19. The category is emptied; then He alone refills it. The ḍamma is the affirmation.

Iʿrāb — word by word لَاnegation of the genus · لَا النَّافِيَة لِلْجِنْس— إِلٰهَits noun — bare fatḥa, no tanwīn · اِسْم لَامَنْصُوب إِلَّاexception · أَدَاة اِسْتِثْنَاء— اللهُsubstitute for the deleted predicate · بَدَلمَرْفُوع
10
الْحَمْدُ لِـ لهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ.

All praise is for Allah, Lord of the worlds.

Ch 1 + Ch 4. A nominal sentence whose predicate is a prepositional phrase, followed by a nested iḍāfa. Four kasras in a row, every one of them explained.

Iʿrāb — word by word الْحَمْدُthe topic · مُبْتَدَأمَرْفُوع لِـpreposition · حَرْف جَرّ— لهِafter the preposition — the predicate · خَبَر · شِبْه جُمْلَةمَجْرُور رَبِّin apposition to اللهِ · بَدَل · مُضَافمَجْرُور الْعَالَمِينَpossessed by رَبِّ · مُضَاف إِلَيْهِمَجْرُور

Section 3The Six Sounds That Carry Your Listening

If you retain nothing else from twenty chapters, retain these. They are what let you parse speech in real time, without stopping.

What you hearWhat it means, instantly
-u at the end of a nounthis is the doer, or the topic. Hold onto it — the sentence is about this.
-a at the end of a nounan object, or an extra — how, when, why, in what state.
-i at the end of a nounafter a preposition, or possessed. Subordinate. Do not over-weight it.
yu- on a verb prefixa derived form — Forms II, III, IV, or a passive. Not a plain verb.
u–i inside a past verba passive. The doer has been deliberately withheld. Ask why.
a clipped, vowel-less verb endingjazm — so لَمْ (past negative), a command, or a conditional.
This is what fluent listening actually is

Not translating. Predicting. A fluent listener hears -u and immediately knows a subject has arrived; hears إِنَّ and is already expecting a fatḥa; hears أَمَّا and braces for a list.

Every ending you can hear in advance is a fraction of a second your brain does not have to spend, and those fractions are the entire difference between following a lecture and drowning in one.

Section 4An Unedited Page — Sūrat al-ʿAṣr

وَالْعَصْرِ ﴿١﴾ إِنَّ الْإِنْسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ ﴿٢﴾ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ ﴿٣﴾

By the passage of time. Indeed, mankind is surely in loss — except those who believed, and did righteous deeds, and enjoined one another to truth, and enjoined one another to patience.

WordRoleChapter
وَالْعَصْرِoath — وَاو القَسَم + a noun in jarr20
إِنَّemphasis — lowers the noun into naṣb6
الْإِنْسَانَاِسْم إِنَّ — naṣb6
لَفِي خُسْرٍemphatic lām + prepositional predicate in jarr20 · 4
إِلَّاexception19
الَّذِينَrelative pronoun, masc. plural8
آمَنُواForm IV, past, 3rd plural9
الصَّالِحَاتِobject — feminine plural, so kasra even in naṣb11
تَوَاصَوْاForm VI — the mutual mould. They enjoined one another.10
بِالْحَقِّpreposition + jarr4
Three verses, seven chapters

Al-Shāfiʿī said that if Allah had revealed nothing but this sūrah, it would have sufficed. He meant it theologically. But look at it grammatically and the same thing is true: three verses, and they use the oath, إِنَّ, the emphatic lām, the exception, the relative clause, Form IV, Form VI, the feminine plural, and the iḍāfa.

Twenty days ago this was calligraphy. Today you can account for every letter of it.

Section 5Listening — Full Speed, No Simplification

الشَّيْخُ:

مَضَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ثَلَاثَةُ أَسَابِيعَ. وَقَدْ تَعَلَّمْتُمُ النَّحْوَ كُلَّهُ. نَعَمْ، كُلَّهُ.

وَلَا تَظُنُّوا أَنِّي أُبَالِغُ. الْجُمْلَةُ الِاسْمِيَّةُ، وَالْفِعْلِيَّةُ، وَالْإِعْرَابُ، وَالْأَوْزَانُ، وَالْجُمُوعُ، وَالشَّرْطُ، وَالْمَجْهُولُ، وَالْحَالُ، وَالِاسْتِثْنَاءُ. مَا بَقِيَ شَيْءٌ.

فَلِمَاذَا لَا تَفْهَمُونَ الْمُحَاضَرَةَ كَامِلَةً؟ لِأَنَّ الْكَلِمَاتِ قَلِيلَةٌ، لَا لِأَنَّ النَّحْوَ نَاقِصٌ.

وَالْكَلِمَاتُ لَا تُدْرَسُ. الْكَلِمَاتُ تُسْمَعُ، مَرَّةً بَعْدَ مَرَّةٍ، حَتَّىٰ تَصِيرَ جُزْءًا مِنْكَ.

فَاذْهَبُوا الْآنَ، وَاسْتَمِعُوا. وَلَا تَنْتَظِرُوا حَتَّىٰ تَفْهَمُوا. اِسْتَمِعُوا لِتَفْهَمُوا.

Shaykh: Three weeks have passed over you. And you have learned all of the grammar. Yes — all of it. And do not think I am exaggerating. The nominal sentence, the verbal sentence, iʿrāb, the verb moulds, the plurals, the conditional, the passive, the ḥāl, the exception. Nothing remains.

So why do you not yet understand a full lecture? Because your words are few — not because your grammar is incomplete.

And words are not studied. Words are heard, time after time, until they become a part of you.

So go now, and listen. And do not wait until you understand. Listen in order to understand.

New: أَسَابِيع weeks · النَّحْو grammar · أُبَالِغُ I exaggerate · الْأَوْزَان the moulds · نَاقِص incomplete · جُزْء a part · لَا تَنْتَظِرُوا do not wait

Section 6Where Your Vocabulary Stands

About 300 words — and, far more importantly, about 90 roots, each of which is now generating four to ten words for you automatically.

ع – ل – مten words from one root
عَلِمَ · عِلْم · عَالِم · عُلَمَاء · مَعْلُوم · عَلَّمَ · مُعَلِّم · تَعَلَّمَ · تَعْلِيم · الْعَلِيم
ش – ه – دfrom Chapter 19
شَهِدَ · شَهَادَة · شَاهِد · شَهِيد · مَشْهُود · أَشْهَدُ
ح – م – دand the names it makes
حَمِدَ · حَمْد · حَامِد · مَحْمُود · مُحَمَّد · أَحْمَد
The honest arithmetic

300 words gets you roughly 50% coverage of running speech. Comfortable comprehension needs about 95%. That gap is not another 300 words — it is another 2,500.

This is not discouraging; it is clarifying. The grammar took three weeks because grammar is finite. Vocabulary will take six to nine months because vocabulary is large. But it is not hard. Twenty new words a day, every day, in a spaced-repetition system, and you arrive.

The wall you were afraid of has already been climbed. What is left is a road, and roads only require walking.

Section 7What the Last Nine Chapters Will Do

ChRegisterWhat changes
22The Qur'anWord order that breaks the rules on purpose. Ellipsis. The absolute object.
23HadithThe isnād as a grammatical object. حَدَّثَنَا, عَنْ, and the technical formulae.
24The lectureFillers, discourse markers, and how a shaykh signposts an argument.
25Fiqh & tafsīrThe technical vocabulary — and how to derive it rather than memorise it.
26–29Real textsUnedited pages, parsed. A real lecture transcript, decoded.
30The road outExactly what to do on Day 31, and for the year after it.
Three weeks in

The shaykh said the thing that matters, so let it stand as the closing line of the first half of your course:

Do not wait until you understand. Listen in order to understand.

End of Chapter 21Next → The Register of the Qur'an
Day 22 · Register: the Qur'an

The Register
of the Qur'an

The grammar is finished. What changes now is how it is used. And Qur'anic Arabic uses it in ways that look, at first, like violations — words in the wrong place, sentences with pieces missing, questions that are not questions. None of it is irregular. All of it is deliberate.

Section 1Objectives

  1. Recognise fronting (التَّقْدِيم) — a word moved out of position, and what that movement means.
  2. Recognise ellipsis (الحَذْف) — a word deliberately deleted, and why the silence is louder than the word.
  3. Learn the absolute object (الْمَفْعُول الْمُطْلَق) — a verb intensified by its own maṣdar.
  4. Stop mistranslating the rhetorical question, which in the Qur'an is almost never a question.
The colour code — the same in every chapter
verb الفِعْل rafʿ مَرْفُوع naṣb مَنْصُوب jarr مَجْرُور particle حَرْف

The colours mark case, not part of speech. Teal is anything in rafʿ — the doer, the topic, the thing said about it. Blue is anything in naṣb — the object, and every "extra": how, when, why, how much. Purple is jarr — after a preposition, or possessed by the word before it. Red is the verb; grey is a particle, which is frozen and has no case at all.

What this chapter is not

This is not tafsīr. It is not theology. It is a chapter about syntax under pressure — what happens to Arabic grammar when a text is trying to say something with maximum force in minimum words.

Every device here also appears in classical poetry and in high oratory. The Qur'an did not invent them. It uses them at a density nothing else reaches.

Section 2Vocabulary — 15 Core Words

01تَلَا
he recited; he followed
verb · defectiveت – ل – وmaṣdar تِلَاوَة
يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِهِ.

He recites His verses to them.

02تَدَبَّرَ
he reflected deeply, he pondered
verb · Form Vد – ب – ر
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْآنَ؟

Do they not reflect upon the Qur'an?

From دُبُر, the back of a thing. Tadabbur is turning a verse over and looking at what stands behind it.

03رَتَّلَ
he recited slowly and distinctly
verb · Form IIر – ت – لmaṣdar تَرْتِيل
وَرَتِّلِ الْقُرْآنَ تَرْتِيلًا.

And recite the Qur'an with measured recitation.

04الْآيَةُ
the verse; a sign
noun · fem.plural آيَات
وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّمَاوَاتِ.
